There is a considerable fluctuation in day-to-day weather and the arrival of a particular season can differ each year.[[194]](#cite_note-Korzeniewska_2020-198) [Climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change") and other factors have further contributed to interannual [thermal anomalies](/wiki/Temperature_anomaly "Temperature anomaly") and increased temperatures; the average annual air temperature between 2011 and 2020 was 9.33 °C (48.8 °F), around 1.11 °C higher than in the 2001–2010 period.[[196]](#cite_note-Anna_Chodubska_2020-200) Winters are also becoming increasingly drier, with less [snowfall](/wiki/Snowfall "Snowfall").[[194]](#cite_note-Korzeniewska_2020-198)

### Biodiversity

Main article: [Geography of Poland § Biodiversity](/wiki/Geography_of_Poland#Biodiversity "Geography of Poland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/39/2020_%C5%BCubry_03.jpg/250px-2020_%C5%BCubry_03.jpg)](/wiki/File:2020_%C5%BCubry_03.jpg)

The [European bison](/wiki/European_bison "European bison") (*żubr*), one of [Poland's national animals](/wiki/National_symbols_of_Poland "National symbols of Poland"), is commonly found at the ancient and [UNESCO](/wiki/List_of_World_Heritage_Sites_in_Poland "List of World Heritage Sites in Poland")-protected [Białowieża Forest](/wiki/Bia%C5%82owie%C5%BCa_Forest "Białowieża Forest").

[Phytogeographically](/wiki/Phytogeography "Phytogeography"), Poland belongs to the Central European province of the [Circumboreal Region](/wiki/Circumboreal_Region "Circumboreal Region") within the [Boreal Kingdom](/wiki/Boreal_Kingdom "Boreal Kingdom"). The country has four [Palearctic ecoregions](/wiki/Palearctic_realm "Palearctic realm") – Central, Northern, Western European [temperate broadleaf and mixed forest](/wiki/Temperate_broadleaf_and_mixed_forest "Temperate broadleaf and mixed forest"), and the [Carpathian montane conifer](/wiki/Carpathian_montane_conifer_forests "Carpathian montane conifer forests"). Forests occupy 31% of Poland's land area, the largest of which is the [Lower Silesian Wilderness](/wiki/Lower_Silesian_Wilderness "Lower Silesian Wilderness").[[197]](#cite_note-201) The most common [deciduous trees](/wiki/Deciduous_trees "Deciduous trees") found across the country are [oak](/wiki/Oak "Oak"), [maple](/wiki/Maple "Maple"), and [beech](/wiki/Beech "Beech"); the most common conifers are [pine](/wiki/Pine "Pine"), [spruce](/wiki/Spruce "Spruce"), and [fir](/wiki/Fir "Fir").[[198]](#cite_note-202) An estimated 69% of all forests are [coniferous](/wiki/Coniferous "Coniferous").[[199]](#cite_note-203)

The [flora](/wiki/Flora "Flora") and [fauna](/wiki/Fauna_of_Poland "Fauna of Poland") in Poland is that of [Continental Europe](/wiki/Continental_Europe "Continental Europe"), with the [wisent](/wiki/European_bison "European bison"), [white stork](/wiki/White_stork "White stork") and [white-tailed eagle](/wiki/White-tailed_eagle "White-tailed eagle") designated as national animals, and the [red common poppy](/wiki/Papaver_rhoeas "Papaver rhoeas") being the unofficial floral emblem.[[200]](#cite_note-204) Among the most protected species is the [European bison](/wiki/European_bison "European bison"), Europe's heaviest land animal, as well as the [Eurasian beaver](/wiki/Eurasian_beaver "Eurasian beaver"), the [lynx](/wiki/Eurasian_lynx "Eurasian lynx"), the [gray wolf](/wiki/Gray_wolf "Gray wolf") and the [Tatra chamois](/wiki/Tatra_chamois "Tatra chamois").[[183]](#cite_note-Rocznik_2019-187) The region was also home to the extinct [aurochs](/wiki/Aurochs "Aurochs"), the last individual dying in Poland in 1627.[[201]](#cite_note-205) Game animals such as [red deer](/wiki/Red_deer "Red deer"), [roe deer](/wiki/Roe_deer "Roe deer"), and [wild boar](/wiki/Wild_boar "Wild boar") are found in most woodlands.[[202]](#cite_note-206) Poland is also a significant breeding ground for [migratory birds](/wiki/Migratory_birds "Migratory birds") and hosts around one quarter of the global population of white storks.[[203]](#cite_note-207)

Around 315,100 hectares (1,217 sq mi), equivalent to 1% of Poland's territory, is protected within 23 [Polish national parks](/wiki/List_of_national_parks_of_Poland "List of national parks of Poland"), two of which – [Białowieża](/wiki/Bia%C5%82owie%C5%BCa_Forest "Białowieża Forest") and [Bieszczady](/wiki/Bieszczady_National_Park "Bieszczady National Park") – are [UNESCO World Heritage Sites](/wiki/List_of_World_Heritage_Sites_in_Poland "List of World Heritage Sites in Poland").[[204]](#cite_note-208) There are 123 areas designated as [landscape parks](/wiki/Landscape_Park_(Poland) "Landscape Park (Poland)"), along with numerous [nature reserves](/wiki/Nature_reserve "Nature reserve") and other [protected areas](/wiki/Protected_areas_of_Poland "Protected areas of Poland") under the [Natura 2000](/wiki/Natura_2000 "Natura 2000") network.[[205]](#cite_note-209)

## Government and politics

Main article: [Politics of Poland](/wiki/Politics_of_Poland "Politics of Poland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dd/Karol_Nawrocki_%282025%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Karol_Nawrocki_%282025%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Karol_Nawrocki_(2025)_(cropped).jpg)

[Karol Nawrocki](/wiki/Karol_Nawrocki "Karol Nawrocki")  
[President](/wiki/President_of_Poland "President of Poland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/Donald_Tusk_2025_%282%29.jpg/250px-Donald_Tusk_2025_%282%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Donald_Tusk_2025_(2).jpg)

[Donald Tusk](/wiki/Donald_Tusk "Donald Tusk")  
[Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Poland "Prime Minister of Poland")

Poland is a [unitary](/wiki/Unitary_state "Unitary state") [parliamentary republic](/wiki/Parliamentary_republic "Parliamentary republic")[[4]](#cite_note-PARLIAMENTARY-5) and a [representative democracy](/wiki/Representative_democracy "Representative democracy"), with [a president](/wiki/President_of_the_Republic_of_Poland "President of the Republic of Poland") as the [head of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state").[[210]](#cite_note-Serwis_Rzeczypospolitej_Polskiej-214) The executive power is exercised further by the [Council of Ministers](/wiki/Council_of_Ministers_of_the_Republic_of_Poland "Council of Ministers of the Republic of Poland") and the [prime minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_the_Republic_of_Poland "Prime Minister of the Republic of Poland") who acts as the [head of government](/wiki/Head_of_government "Head of government").[[210]](#cite_note-Serwis_Rzeczypospolitej_Polskiej-214) The council's individual members are selected by the prime minister, approved by parliament and sworn in by the president.[[210]](#cite_note-Serwis_Rzeczypospolitej_Polskiej-214) The head of state is elected by [popular vote](/wiki/Direct_election "Direct election") for a five-year term.[[211]](#cite_note-215) The current president is [Karol Nawrocki](/wiki/Karol_Nawrocki "Karol Nawrocki")[[212]](#cite_note-216) and the prime minister is [Donald Tusk](/wiki/Donald_Tusk "Donald Tusk").

Poland's [legislative](/wiki/Legislature "Legislature") assembly is a [bicameral](/wiki/Bicameralism "Bicameralism") parliament consisting of a 460-member lower house ([Sejm](/wiki/Sejm "Sejm")) and a 100-member upper house ([Senate](/wiki/Senate_of_the_Republic_of_Poland "Senate of the Republic of Poland")).[[213]](#cite_note-217) The Sejm is elected under [proportional representation](/wiki/Proportional_representation "Proportional representation") according to the [d'Hondt method](/wiki/D%27Hondt_method "D'Hondt method") for vote-seat conversion.[[214]](#cite_note-218) The Senate is elected under the [first-past-the-post](/wiki/First-past-the-post "First-past-the-post") electoral system, with one senator being returned from each of the one hundred constituencies.[[215]](#cite_note-Granat_2021-219) The Senate has the right to amend or reject a statute passed by the Sejm, but the Sejm may override the Senate's decision with a majority vote.[[216]](#cite_note-220)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/2024-09_Sejm_RP_Sala_Posiedze%C5%84_%284%29.jpg/250px-2024-09_Sejm_RP_Sala_Posiedze%C5%84_%284%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:2024-09_Sejm_RP_Sala_Posiedze%C5%84_(4).jpg)

The [Sejm](/wiki/Sejm "Sejm") is the [lower house](/wiki/Lower_house "Lower house") of the [parliament of Poland](/wiki/Parliament_of_Poland "Parliament of Poland").

With the exception of ethnic minority parties, only candidates of [political parties](/wiki/List_of_political_parties_in_Poland "List of political parties in Poland") receiving at least 5% of the total national vote can enter the Sejm.[[215]](#cite_note-Granat_2021-219) Both the lower and upper houses of parliament in Poland are elected for a four-year term and each member of the Polish parliament is guaranteed [parliamentary immunity](/wiki/Parliamentary_immunity "Parliamentary immunity").[[217]](#cite_note-Jasinski_2019-221) Under current legislation, a person must be 21 years of age or over to assume the position of deputy, 30 or over to become senator and 35 to run in a presidential election.[[217]](#cite_note-Jasinski_2019-221)

Members of the Sejm and Senate jointly form the [National Assembly of the Republic of Poland](/wiki/National_Assembly_of_the_Republic_of_Poland "National Assembly of the Republic of Poland").[[218]](#cite_note-Bien-Kacala_2021-222) The National Assembly, headed by the [marshal of the Sejm](/wiki/Marshal_of_the_Sejm "Marshal of the Sejm"), or [marshal of the Senate](/wiki/Marshal_of_the_Senate "Marshal of the Senate") in their absence, is formed on three occasions – when a new president takes the [oath of office](/wiki/Oath_of_office "Oath of office"); when an indictment against the president is brought to the [State Tribunal](/wiki/State_Tribunal_(Poland) "State Tribunal (Poland)"); and in case a president's permanent incapacity to exercise his duties due to the state of his health is declared.[[218]](#cite_note-Bien-Kacala_2021-222)

According to [International IDEA's](/wiki/International_Institute_for_Democracy_and_Electoral_Assistance "International Institute for Democracy and Electoral Assistance") Global State of Democracy (GSoD) Indices and Democracy Tracker, Poland performs in the mid-range on overall democratic measures, with particular weaknesses in civic engagement and judicial independence.[[219]](#cite_note-223)[[220]](#cite_note-224)[[221]](#cite_note-225)

### Administrative divisions

Main article: [Administrative divisions of Poland](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_Poland "Administrative divisions of Poland")

Poland is divided into 16 provinces or states known as [voivodeships](/wiki/Voivodeships_of_Poland "Voivodeships of Poland").[[222]](#cite_note-Teryt_2022-226) As of 2022, the voivodeships are subdivided into 380 counties (*[powiats](/wiki/Powiat "Powiat")*), which are further fragmented into 2,477 municipalities (*[gminas](/wiki/Gmina "Gmina")*).[[222]](#cite_note-Teryt_2022-226) Major cities normally have the status of both *gmina* and *powiat*.[[222]](#cite_note-Teryt_2022-226) The provinces are largely founded on the borders of [historic regions](/wiki/Polish_historical_regions "Polish historical regions"), or named for individual cities.[[223]](#cite_note-Marti-Henneberg_2021-227) Administrative authority at the voivodeship level is shared between a government-appointed governor ([voivode](/wiki/Voivodes_of_Poland_(since_1999) "Voivodes of Poland (since 1999)")), an elected regional assembly ([sejmik](/wiki/Voivodeship_sejmik "Voivodeship sejmik")) and a [voivodeship marshal](/wiki/Voivodeship_marshal "Voivodeship marshal"), an executive elected by the assembly.[[223]](#cite_note-Marti-Henneberg_2021-227)

### Law

Main article: [Law of Poland](/wiki/Law_of_Poland "Law of Poland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9d/Manuscript_of_the_Constitution_of_the_3rd_May_1791.PNG/250px-Manuscript_of_the_Constitution_of_the_3rd_May_1791.PNG)](/wiki/File:Manuscript_of_the_Constitution_of_the_3rd_May_1791.PNG)

The [Constitution of 3 May](/wiki/Constitution_of_May_3,_1791 "Constitution of May 3, 1791") adopted in 1791 was the first modern constitution in Europe.

The [Constitution of Poland](/wiki/Constitution_of_Poland "Constitution of Poland") is the enacted supreme law, and Polish judicature is based on the principle of civil rights, governed by the code of [civil law](/wiki/Civil_law_(legal_system) "Civil law (legal system)").[[225]](#cite_note-229) The current democratic constitution was adopted by the [National Assembly of Poland](/wiki/National_Assembly_of_Poland "National Assembly of Poland") on 2 April 1997; it guarantees a [multi-party state](/wiki/Multi-party_state "Multi-party state") with freedoms of religion, speech and gatherings, prohibits the practices of forced [medical experimentation](/wiki/Medical_experimentation "Medical experimentation"), torture or [corporal punishment](/wiki/Corporal_punishment "Corporal punishment"), and acknowledges the inviolability of the home, the right to form trade unions, and the right to [strike](/wiki/Strike_action "Strike action").[[226]](#cite_note-230)

The [judiciary](/wiki/Judiciary_of_Poland "Judiciary of Poland") in Poland is composed of the [Supreme Court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_Poland "Supreme Court of Poland") as the country's highest judicial organ, the [Supreme Administrative Court](/wiki/Supreme_Administrative_Court_of_Poland "Supreme Administrative Court of Poland") for the judicial control of public administration, Common Courts ([District](/wiki/District_Court "District Court"), [Regional](/wiki/Regional_Court_(Poland) "Regional Court (Poland)"), [Appellate](/wiki/Appellate_court "Appellate court")) and the [Military Court](/wiki/Court-martial "Court-martial").[[227]](#cite_note-231) The [Constitutional](/wiki/Constitutional_Tribunal_(Poland) "Constitutional Tribunal (Poland)") and State Tribunals are separate judicial bodies, which rule the constitutional liability of people holding the highest offices of state and supervise the compliance of [statutory law](/wiki/Statutory_law "Statutory law"), thus protecting the Constitution.[[228]](#cite_note-Jaremba_2013-232) Judges are nominated by the [National Council of the Judiciary](/wiki/National_Council_of_the_Judiciary "National Council of the Judiciary") and are appointed for life by the [president](/wiki/President_of_Poland "President of Poland").[[228]](#cite_note-Jaremba_2013-232) With the approval of the Senate, the Sejm appoints an [ombudsman](/wiki/Ombudsman "Ombudsman") for a five-year term to guard the observance of social justice.[[215]](#cite_note-Granat_2021-219)

Poland has a low [homicide](/wiki/Homicide "Homicide") rate at 0.7 murders per 100,000 people, as of 2018.[[229]](#cite_note-:0-233) Rape, assault and violent crime remain at a very low level.[[230]](#cite_note-234) The country has imposed strict regulations on [abortion](/wiki/Abortion_in_Poland "Abortion in Poland"), which is permitted only in cases of [rape](/wiki/Rape "Rape"), [incest](/wiki/Incest "Incest") or when the woman's life is in danger; [congenital disorder](/wiki/Congenital_disorder "Congenital disorder") is not covered by the law, prompting some women to seek abortion abroad.[[231]](#cite_note-235)

Historically, the most significant Polish legal act is the [Constitution of 3 May 1791](/wiki/Constitution_of_3_May_1791 "Constitution of 3 May 1791"). Instituted to redress long-standing political defects of the [federative](/wiki/Federation "Federation") [Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth](/wiki/Polish%E2%80%93Lithuanian_Commonwealth "Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth") and its [Golden Liberty](/wiki/Golden_Liberty "Golden Liberty"), it was the first modern constitution in Europe and influenced many later [democratic movements](/wiki/Democratic_movement "Democratic movement") across the globe.[[232]](#cite_note-Davies_1996-236)[[233]](#cite_note-237) In 1918, the [Second Polish Republic](/wiki/Second_Polish_Republic "Second Polish Republic") became one of the first countries to introduce universal [women's suffrage](/wiki/Women%27s_suffrage "Women's suffrage").[[234]](#cite_note-238)

### Foreign relations

Main articles: [Foreign relations of Poland](/wiki/Foreign_relations_of_Poland "Foreign relations of Poland") and [List of diplomatic missions of Poland](/wiki/List_of_diplomatic_missions_of_Poland "List of diplomatic missions of Poland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/Ministerstwo_Spraw_Zagraniczych_al._Szucha_23.JPG/250px-Ministerstwo_Spraw_Zagraniczych_al._Szucha_23.JPG)](/wiki/File:Ministerstwo_Spraw_Zagraniczych_al._Szucha_23.JPG)

The [Ministry of Foreign Affairs](/wiki/Ministry_of_Foreign_Affairs_(Poland) "Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Poland)"), located in [Warsaw](/wiki/Warsaw "Warsaw")

Poland is a [middle power](/wiki/Middle_power "Middle power") and a [regional power](/wiki/Regional_power "Regional power") in Europe.[[235]](#cite_note-239)[[236]](#cite_note-240) It has a total of 53 representatives in the [European Parliament](/wiki/European_Parliament "European Parliament") as of 2024. [Warsaw](/wiki/Warsaw "Warsaw") serves as the headquarters for [Frontex](/wiki/Frontex "Frontex"), the European Union's agency for external border security as well as [ODIHR](/wiki/ODIHR "ODIHR"), one of the principal institutions of the [OSCE](/wiki/Organization_for_Security_and_Co-operation_in_Europe "Organization for Security and Co-operation in Europe").[[237]](#cite_note-241)[[238]](#cite_note-242) Apart from the European Union, Poland has been a member of [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO"), the United Nations, and the [WTO](/wiki/World_Trade_Organization "World Trade Organization").

In recent years, Poland significantly strengthened its [relations](/wiki/Poland%E2%80%93United_States_relations "Poland–United States relations") with the United States, becoming one of its closest [allies](/wiki/Alliance "Alliance") and strategic partners in Europe.[[239]](#cite_note-243) Historically, Poland maintained strong [cultural and political](/wiki/Hungary%E2%80%93Poland_relations "Hungary–Poland relations") ties with Hungary.[[240]](#cite_note-244)

### Military

Main article: [Polish Armed Forces](/wiki/Polish_Armed_Forces "Polish Armed Forces")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/81/F-16_Jastrz%C4%85b_%2848%29.jpg/250px-F-16_Jastrz%C4%85b_%2848%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:F-16_Jastrz%C4%85b_(48).jpg)

[Polish Air Force](/wiki/Polish_Air_Force "Polish Air Force") [F-16s](/wiki/F-16_Fighting_Falcon "F-16 Fighting Falcon"), a single-engine [multirole](/wiki/Multirole_combat_aircraft "Multirole combat aircraft") [fighter aircraft](/wiki/Fighter_aircraft "Fighter aircraft")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/BWP_Borsuk_-_221114-Z-YU201-1007.jpg/250px-BWP_Borsuk_-_221114-Z-YU201-1007.jpg)](/wiki/File:BWP_Borsuk_-_221114-Z-YU201-1007.jpg)

The [amphibious](/wiki/Amphibious_vehicle "Amphibious vehicle") [infantry fighting vehicle](/wiki/Infantry_fighting_vehicle "Infantry fighting vehicle") [Borsuk](/wiki/Borsuk_(infantry_fighting_vehicle) "Borsuk (infantry fighting vehicle)")

The Polish Armed Forces are composed of five branches – the [Land Forces](/wiki/Polish_Land_Forces "Polish Land Forces"), the [Navy](/wiki/Polish_Navy "Polish Navy"), the [Air Force](/wiki/Polish_Air_Force "Polish Air Force"), the [Special Forces](/wiki/Special_forces_of_Poland "Special forces of Poland") and the [Territorial Defence Force](/wiki/Territorial_Defence_Force_(Poland) "Territorial Defence Force (Poland)").[[241]](#cite_note-Mihalcova_2019-245) The military is subordinate to the [Ministry of National Defence of the Republic of Poland](/wiki/Ministry_of_National_Defence_of_the_Republic_of_Poland "Ministry of National Defence of the Republic of Poland").[[241]](#cite_note-Mihalcova_2019-245) However, its commander-in-chief in peacetime is the president, who nominates officers, the Minister for National Defence and the chief of staff.[[241]](#cite_note-Mihalcova_2019-245) Polish military tradition is generally commemorated by the [Armed Forces Day](/wiki/Armed_Forces_Day_(Poland) "Armed Forces Day (Poland)"), celebrated annually on 15 August.[[242]](#cite_note-246) In July 2024, the Polish Armed Forces had a combined strength of 216,100 active soldiers, making it the largest standing army in the European Union and the third largest in [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO").[[243]](#cite_note-Celej-247)

Poland ranks [14th in the world](/wiki/List_of_countries_with_highest_military_expenditures "List of countries with highest military expenditures") in terms of military expenditures; the country allocated 4.12% of its total GDP on military spending, equivalent to approximately US$35 billion in 2024.[[244]](#cite_note-ENR-248) From 2022, Poland initiated a programme of mass modernisation of its armed forces, in close cooperation with American, South Korean and local Polish [defence manufacturers](/wiki/Arms_industry "Arms industry")[[245]](#cite_note-249) concentrated in the [Polish Armaments Group](/wiki/Polish_Armaments_Group "Polish Armaments Group"). Also, the Polish military is set to increase its size to 250,000 enlisted and officers, and 50,000 Territorial Defence Force.[[246]](#cite_note-250) According to [SIPRI](/wiki/SIPRI "SIPRI"), the country exported €487 million worth of arms and armaments to foreign countries in 2020.[[247]](#cite_note-251)

Compulsory [military service](/wiki/Conscription "Conscription") for men, was discontinued in 2008.[[248]](#cite_note-Day_2008-252) Polish military doctrine reflects the same defensive nature as that of its NATO partners and the country actively hosts NATO's [military exercises](/wiki/Military_exercises "Military exercises").[[249]](#cite_note-IISS_2022-253) The Polish Special Forces, consisting of six units ([Grom](/wiki/GROM_Military_Unit "GROM Military Unit"), [Formoza](/wiki/Formoza_Military_Unit "Formoza Military Unit"), [Agat](/wiki/Agat_Military_Unit "Agat Military Unit"), [Nil](/wiki/Nil_Military_Unit "Nil Military Unit"), [Commandos Military Unit](/wiki/Commandos_Military_Unit "Commandos Military Unit"), [Airborne Special Operations Unit](https://pl.wikipedia.org/wiki/Powietrzna_Jednostka_Operacji_Specjalnych "pl:Powietrzna Jednostka Operacji Specjalnych")), have the authority to command full-scale as well as joint NATO operations, with special forces from all NATO member states.[[250]](#cite_note-254) Since 1953, the country has been a large contributor to various United Nations peacekeeping missions,[[251]](#cite_note-255) and currently maintains military presence in the Middle East, Africa, the [Baltic states](/wiki/Baltic_states "Baltic states") and southeastern Europe.[[249]](#cite_note-IISS_2022-253) Moreover, there are approximately 10,000 troops from the [United States Armed Forces](/wiki/United_States_Armed_Forces "United States Armed Forces") stationed across Poland.[[252]](#cite_note-DepartmentofState-256) From 2024, Poland also provides mandatory [firearms training](/wiki/Firearms_training "Firearms training") for pupils in [primary](/wiki/Primary_school "Primary school") and [secondary schools](/wiki/Secondary_school "Secondary school").[[253]](#cite_note-PAPpl-257)

### Security, law enforcement and emergency services

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/Policja_-_Police_in_Warsaw%2C_Toyota_Auris_%282019%29.jpg/250px-Policja_-_Police_in_Warsaw%2C_Toyota_Auris_%282019%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Policja_-_Police_in_Warsaw,_Toyota_Auris_(2019).jpg)

A [Toyota Auris](/wiki/Toyota_Auris "Toyota Auris") patrol car belonging to the Polish [State Police Service](/wiki/Policja "Policja") (*Policja*)

Main articles: [Law enforcement in Poland](/wiki/Law_enforcement_in_Poland "Law enforcement in Poland"), [Emergency medical services in Poland](/wiki/Emergency_medical_services_in_Poland "Emergency medical services in Poland"), and [State Fire Service](/wiki/State_Fire_Service "State Fire Service")

Law enforcement in Poland is performed by several agencies which are subordinate to the [Ministry of Interior and Administration](/wiki/Ministry_of_Interior_and_Administration_(Poland) "Ministry of Interior and Administration (Poland)") – the [State Police](/wiki/Policja "Policja") (*Policja*), assigned to investigate crimes or transgression; the [Municipal City Guard](/wiki/City_Guard_(Poland) "City Guard (Poland)"), which maintains [public order](/wiki/Public-order_crime "Public-order crime"); and several specialised agencies, such as the [Polish Border Guard](/wiki/Polish_Border_Guard "Polish Border Guard").[[254]](#cite_note-Narodowego_2013-258) Municipal guards are primarily headed by provincial, regional or city councils; individual guards are not permitted to carry [firearms](/wiki/Firearms "Firearms") unless instructed by the superior commanding officer.[[255]](#cite_note-259) Security service personnel conduct regular patrols in both large urban areas or smaller suburban localities.[[256]](#cite_note-260)

Emergency services in Poland consist of the [emergency medical services](/wiki/Emergency_medical_services_in_Poland "Emergency medical services in Poland"), [search and rescue](/wiki/Search_and_rescue#Poland "Search and rescue") units of the [Polish Armed Forces](/wiki/Polish_Armed_Forces "Polish Armed Forces") and [State Fire Service](/wiki/State_Fire_Service "State Fire Service"). Emergency medical services in Poland are operated by local and regional governments,[[257]](#cite_note-261) but are a part of the centralised national agency – the [National Medical Emergency Service](/wiki/Emergency_medical_services_in_Poland "Emergency medical services in Poland") (*Państwowe Ratownictwo Medyczne*).[[258]](#cite_note-262) Thanks to its location, Poland is a country essentially free from the threat of natural disasters such as [earthquakes](/wiki/Earthquakes "Earthquakes"), [volcanic eruptions](/wiki/Volcanic_eruptions "Volcanic eruptions"), [tornadoes](/wiki/Tornadoes "Tornadoes") and [tropical cyclones](/wiki/Tropical_cyclones "Tropical cyclones"). However, [floods](/wiki/Flood "Flood") have occurred in low-lying areas from time to time during periods of extreme rainfall, for example during the [2010 Central European floods](/wiki/2010_Central_European_floods "2010 Central European floods").

The [Internal Security Agency](/wiki/Internal_Security_Agency "Internal Security Agency") (ABW, or ISA in English) is the chief [counterintelligence instrument](/wiki/Intelligence_agency "Intelligence agency") safeguarding Poland's internal security, along with [Agencja Wywiadu](/wiki/Agencja_Wywiadu "Agencja Wywiadu") (AW) which identifies threats and collects secret information abroad.[[259]](#cite_note-263) The [Central Investigation Bureau of Police](/wiki/Centralne_Biuro_%C5%9Aledcze_Policji "Centralne Biuro Śledcze Policji") (CBŚP) and the [Central Anticorruption Bureau](/wiki/Central_Anticorruption_Bureau "Central Anticorruption Bureau") (CBA) are responsible for countering organised crime and corruption in state and private institutions.[[260]](#cite_note-264)[[261]](#cite_note-265)

## Economy

Main article: [Economy of Poland](/wiki/Economy_of_Poland "Economy of Poland")

**Economic indicators**

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [GDP (PPP)](/wiki/Gross_domestic_product "Gross domestic product") | $2.019 trillion (2025)[[11]](#cite_note-IMFWEO.PL-13) |
| Nominal GDP | $1.039 trillion (2025)[[11]](#cite_note-IMFWEO.PL-13) |
| Real GDP growth | 2.9% (2024)[[262]](#cite_note-266) |
| [CPI](/wiki/Consumer_price_index "Consumer price index") inflation | 2.5% (May 2024)[[263]](#cite_note-267) |
| [Employment-to-population](/wiki/Employment-to-population_ratio "Employment-to-population ratio") | 57% (2022)[[264]](#cite_note-268) |
| Unemployment | 2.8% (2025)[[11]](#cite_note-IMFWEO.PL-13) |
| [Total public debt](/wiki/National_debt "National debt") | $502.3 billion (2024)[[265]](#cite_note-269) |

Poland has a [social market economy](/wiki/Social_market_economy "Social market economy") and is a regional [economic power](/wiki/Economic_power "Economic power") in [East-Central Europe](/wiki/East-Central_Europe "East-Central Europe").[[266]](#cite_note-Kochanski&Partners-270) As of 2025[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Poland&action=edit), the country's gross domestic product (GDP) is the sixth largest in the European Union by [nominal standards](/wiki/Economy_of_the_European_Union "Economy of the European Union"), the fifth largest by [purchasing power parity](/wiki/List_of_sovereign_states_in_Europe_by_GDP_(PPP) "List of sovereign states in Europe by GDP (PPP)"), and ranks [20th globally](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(nominal) "List of countries by GDP (nominal)").[[267]](#cite_note-271) It is one of the fastest growing within the Union and reached a [developed market](/wiki/Developed_market "Developed market") status in 2018.[[268]](#cite_note-272) The unemployment rate published by [Eurostat](/wiki/Eurostat "Eurostat") in 2023 amounted to 2.8%, which was the second-lowest in the EU.[[269]](#cite_note-Ministry_of_Family_and_Social_Policy-273) As of 2023[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Poland&action=edit), around 62% of the employed population works in the [service sector](/wiki/Tertiary_sector_of_the_economy "Tertiary sector of the economy"), 29% in manufacturing, and 8% in the agricultural sector, thus manifesting a highly [diversified economy](/wiki/Economic_diversity "Economic diversity").[[270]](#cite_note-274) Although Poland is a member of the [European single market](/wiki/European_single_market "European single market"), the country has not adopted the [Euro](/wiki/Euro "Euro") as legal tender and maintains its own currency – the [Polish złoty](/wiki/Polish_z%C5%82oty "Polish złoty") (zł, PLN).[[271]](#cite_note-IM-275)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d6/Calle_Dlugie_Pobrzeze%2C_Gdansk%2C_Polonia%2C_2013-05-20%2C_DD_05.jpg/250px-Calle_Dlugie_Pobrzeze%2C_Gdansk%2C_Polonia%2C_2013-05-20%2C_DD_05.jpg)](/wiki/File:Calle_Dlugie_Pobrzeze,_Gdansk,_Polonia,_2013-05-20,_DD_05.jpg)

[Gdańsk](/wiki/Gda%C5%84sk "Gdańsk"), located on the [Baltic](/wiki/Baltic_Sea "Baltic Sea") in the north of Poland, is a major [seaport](/wiki/Seaport "Seaport") as well as an economic and tourist zone.

Poland is a regional European leader in terms of [foreign direct investment](/wiki/Foreign_direct_investment "Foreign direct investment")[[272]](#cite_note-wb-276) and possesses around 40 percent of the 500 biggest companies in the region by revenues whilst maintaining a [high globalisation rate](/wiki/Globalisation_index "Globalisation index") and relatively high economic competitiveness.[[273]](#cite_note-Polish_economy_seen_as_stable_and_competitive-277)[[274]](#cite_note-IMD-278) The country's largest firms compose the [WIG20](/wiki/WIG20 "WIG20") and [WIG30](/wiki/WIG30 "WIG30") [stock market indexes](/wiki/Stock_market_index "Stock market index"), which are traded on the [Warsaw Stock Exchange](/wiki/Warsaw_Stock_Exchange "Warsaw Stock Exchange").[[275]](#cite_note-Zarzecki&Dietl-279) The [Central Statistical Office](/wiki/Central_Statistical_Office_(Poland) "Central Statistical Office (Poland)") estimated that in 2014 there were 1,437 Polish corporations with interests in 3,194 foreign entities.[[276]](#cite_note-280) Poland also has the largest banking sector in Central Europe,[[277]](#cite_note-281) with 32.3 branches per 100,000 adults.[[278]](#cite_note-282) The monetary policy is determined by the [National Bank of Poland](/wiki/National_Bank_of_Poland "National Bank of Poland") (NBP), which controls the issuing of the national currency.[[271]](#cite_note-IM-275) It was the only European economy to have avoided the [recession of 2008](/wiki/Great_Recession "Great Recession").[[279]](#cite_note-Schwab_2011-283) Since 2019, workers under the age of 26 are exempt from paying the [income tax](/wiki/Income_tax "Income tax").[[280]](#cite_note-284)

The country is the [19th largest exporter](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_exports "List of countries by exports") of goods and [services](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_service_exports "List of countries by service exports") in the world.[[281]](#cite_note-285) Exports of goods and services are valued at approximately 58% of GDP, as of 2023.[[282]](#cite_note-286) Poland's largest trade partners are Germany, the United Kingdom, the [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic "Czech Republic"), France, Italy, the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands"), and the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States").[[283]](#cite_note-OEC-287) Among its lead exports are motor cars, buses, and vehicle-related accessories, machinery, [electronics](/wiki/Electronics "Electronics"), [electric batteries](/wiki/Electric_batteries "Electric batteries"), [home appliances](/wiki/Home_appliance "Home appliance"), furniture, cosmetics, military equipment, and [tobacco](/wiki/Tobacco "Tobacco") as well as materials such as silver, [copper](/wiki/Copper "Copper"), [steel](/wiki/Steel "Steel"), [coal](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_coal_production "List of countries by coal production"), [zinc](/wiki/Zinc "Zinc"), [tar](/wiki/Tar "Tar"), and [coke](/wiki/Coke_(fuel) "Coke (fuel)").[[283]](#cite_note-OEC-287) In 2023, the country produced 1300 tonnes of silver and was the 5th largest silver producer globally.[[284]](#cite_note-288) As of 2025, Poland holds the world's 12th largest [gold reserve](/wiki/Gold_reserve "Gold reserve"), estimated at 509 tonnes.[[285]](#cite_note-289)

### Tourism

Main article: [Tourism in Poland](/wiki/Tourism_in_Poland "Tourism in Poland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b1/Malbork_zamek_wysoki_i_sredni_%28dron%29.jpg/250px-Malbork_zamek_wysoki_i_sredni_%28dron%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Malbork_zamek_wysoki_i_sredni_(dron).jpg)

[Malbork Castle](/wiki/Malbork_Castle "Malbork Castle") is the largest castle in the world measured by land area and a [UNESCO World Heritage Site](/wiki/List_of_World_Heritage_Sites_in_Poland "List of World Heritage Sites in Poland").

In 2020, the total value of the [tourism industry](/wiki/Tourism "Tourism") in Poland was 104.3 billion [PLN](/wiki/Polish_z%C5%82oty "Polish złoty"), then equivalent to 4.5% of the Polish GDP.[[286]](#cite_note-oecdtourism-290) Tourism contributes considerably to the overall economy and makes up a relatively large proportion of the country's service market.[[287]](#cite_note-291) Nearly 200,000 people were employed in the [accommodation and catering](/wiki/Hospitality_industry "Hospitality industry") (hospitality) sector in 2020.[[286]](#cite_note-oecdtourism-290) In 2021, Poland ranked [12th most visited country](/wiki/World_Tourism_rankings "World Tourism rankings") in the world by international arrivals.[[288]](#cite_note-unwto-292)

Tourist attractions in Poland vary, from the mountains in the south to the wide sandy beaches Baltic Sea in the north. Many trail of rich architectural and cultural heritage. Among the most recognisable landmarks are Old Towns in [Kraków](/wiki/Krak%C3%B3w_Old_Town "Kraków Old Town"), [Warsaw](/wiki/Warsaw_Old_Town "Warsaw Old Town"), [Wrocław](/wiki/Wroc%C5%82aw "Wrocław") ([dwarf statues](/wiki/Wroc%C5%82aw%27s_dwarfs "Wrocław's dwarfs")), [Gdańsk](/wiki/Gda%C5%84sk "Gdańsk"), [Poznań](/wiki/Pozna%C5%84 "Poznań"), [Lublin](/wiki/Lublin "Lublin"), [Toruń](/wiki/Toru%C5%84 "Toruń") and [Zamość](/wiki/Zamo%C5%9B%C4%87 "Zamość") as well as museums, zoological gardens, theme parks and the [Wieliczka Salt Mine](/wiki/Wieliczka_Salt_Mine "Wieliczka Salt Mine"), with its labyrinthine tunnels, [underground lake](/wiki/Underground_lake "Underground lake") and chapels carved by miners out of [rock salt](/wiki/Rock_salt "Rock salt") beneath the ground. There are [over 100 castles](/wiki/List_of_castles_in_Poland "List of castles in Poland") in the country, largely within the [Lower Silesian Voivodeship](/wiki/Lower_Silesian_Voivodeship "Lower Silesian Voivodeship") (including the Piast Castles Trail), and also on the [Trail of the Eagles' Nests](/wiki/Trail_of_the_Eagles%27_Nests "Trail of the Eagles' Nests"); the largest castle in the world by land area is situated in [Malbork](/wiki/Malbork_Castle "Malbork Castle").[[289]](#cite_note-293)[[290]](#cite_note-294) The German [Auschwitz concentration camp](/wiki/Auschwitz_concentration_camp "Auschwitz concentration camp") in [Oświęcim](/wiki/O%C5%9Bwi%C4%99cim "Oświęcim"), and the [Skull Chapel](/wiki/Skull_Chapel "Skull Chapel") in [Kudowa-Zdrój](/wiki/Kudowa-Zdr%C3%B3j "Kudowa-Zdrój") constitute [dark tourism](/wiki/Dark_tourism "Dark tourism").[[291]](#cite_note-Watts-295) Regarding nature based travel, notable sites include the [Masurian Lake District](/wiki/Masurian_Lake_District "Masurian Lake District") and [Białowieża Forest](/wiki/Bia%C5%82owie%C5%BCa_Forest "Białowieża Forest") in the east; on the south [Karkonosze](/wiki/Karkonosze "Karkonosze"), the [Table Mountains](/wiki/Table_Mountains "Table Mountains") and the [Tatra Mountains](/wiki/Tatra_Mountains "Tatra Mountains"), where [Rysy](/wiki/Rysy "Rysy") and the [Eagle's Path](/wiki/Orla_Per%C4%87 "Orla Perć") trail are located. The [Pieniny](/wiki/Pieniny "Pieniny") and [Bieszczady Mountains](/wiki/Bieszczady_Mountains "Bieszczady Mountains") lie in the extreme south-east.[[292]](#cite_note-UNTWO_2008-296)

### Transport

Main article: [Transport in Poland](/wiki/Transport_in_Poland "Transport in Poland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f8/WK15_Wroc%C5%82aw_G%C5%82%C3%B3wny_%282%29_Lichen99.jpg/250px-WK15_Wroc%C5%82aw_G%C5%82%C3%B3wny_%282%29_Lichen99.jpg)](/wiki/File:WK15_Wroc%C5%82aw_G%C5%82%C3%B3wny_(2)_Lichen99.jpg)

[PKP Intercity](/wiki/Polish_State_Railways "Polish State Railways") [Pendolino](/wiki/New_Pendolino "New Pendolino") at the [Wrocław railway station](/wiki/Wroc%C5%82aw_G%C5%82%C3%B3wny_railway_station "Wrocław Główny railway station")

Transport in Poland is provided by means of [rail](/wiki/Rail_transport_in_Poland "Rail transport in Poland"), [road](/wiki/Highways_in_Poland "Highways in Poland"), [marine shipping](/wiki/Polish_Merchant_Navy "Polish Merchant Navy") and [air travel](/wiki/List_of_airports_in_Poland "List of airports in Poland"). The country is part of EU's [Schengen Area](/wiki/Schengen_Area "Schengen Area") and is an important transport hub due to its strategic geographical position in Central Europe.[[293]](#cite_note-297) Some of the longest European routes, including the [E30](/wiki/European_route_E30 "European route E30") and [E40](/wiki/European_route_E40 "European route E40"), run through Poland. The country has a good network of [highways](/wiki/Highways_in_Poland "Highways in Poland") consisting of [express roads](/wiki/Limited-access_road "Limited-access road") and [motorways](/wiki/Controlled-access_highway "Controlled-access highway"). As of August 2023, Poland has the world's [21st-largest road network](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_road_network_size "List of countries by road network size"), maintaining over 5,000 km (3,100 mi) of highways in use.[[294]](#cite_note-298) In larger cities, [public transport](/wiki/Public_transport "Public transport") is heavily utilised; some of Poland's [tram and light rail transit systems](/wiki/List_of_largest_tram_and_light_rail_transit_systems_ever "List of largest tram and light rail transit systems ever") are among the world's largest, with Europe's biggest rolling stock.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 2022, the nation had 19,393 kilometres (12,050 mi) of railway track, the third longest in the European Union after Germany and France.[[295]](#cite_note-299) The [Polish State Railways](/wiki/Polish_State_Railways "Polish State Railways") (PKP) is the dominant railway operator, with certain major voivodeships or urban areas possessing their own [commuter](/wiki/Commuter_rail "Commuter rail") and [regional rail](/wiki/Regional_rail "Regional rail").[[296]](#cite_note-300) Poland has a number of international airports, the largest of which is [Warsaw Chopin Airport](/wiki/Warsaw_Chopin_Airport "Warsaw Chopin Airport").[[297]](#cite_note-301) It is the primary global hub for [LOT Polish Airlines](/wiki/LOT_Polish_Airlines "LOT Polish Airlines"), the country's [flag carrier](/wiki/Flag_carrier "Flag carrier").[[298]](#cite_note-302)

Seaports exist all along Poland's Baltic coast, with most freight operations using [Świnoujście](/wiki/Port_of_%C5%9Awinouj%C5%9Bcie "Port of Świnoujście"), [Police](/wiki/Port_of_Police "Port of Police"), [Szczecin](/wiki/Port_of_Szczecin "Port of Szczecin"), [Kołobrzeg](/wiki/Port_of_Ko%C5%82obrzeg "Port of Kołobrzeg"), [Gdynia](/wiki/Port_of_Gdynia "Port of Gdynia"), [Gdańsk](/wiki/Port_of_Gda%C5%84sk "Port of Gdańsk") and [Elbląg](/wiki/Elbl%C4%85g "Elbląg") as their base. The [Port of Gdańsk](/wiki/Port_of_Gda%C5%84sk "Port of Gdańsk") is the only port in the [Baltic Sea](/wiki/Baltic_Sea "Baltic Sea") adapted to receive oceanic vessels. [Polferries](/wiki/Polferries "Polferries") and [Unity Line](/wiki/Unity_Line "Unity Line") are the largest Polish ferry operators, with the latter providing [roll-on/roll-off](/wiki/Roll-on/roll-off "Roll-on/roll-off") and [train ferry](/wiki/Train_ferry "Train ferry") services to [Scandinavia](/wiki/Scandinavia "Scandinavia").[[299]](#cite_note-303)

### Energy

Main article: [Energy in Poland](/wiki/Energy_in_Poland "Energy in Poland")

The electricity generation sector in Poland is largely [fossil-fuel](/wiki/Fossil_fuel "Fossil fuel")–based. Coal production in Poland is a major source of employment and the largest source of the nation's [greenhouse gas emissions](/wiki/Greenhouse_gas_emissions "Greenhouse gas emissions").[[300]](#cite_note-International_Energy_Agency_2022-304) Many power plants nationwide use Poland's position as a major European exporter of coal to their advantage by continuing to use coal as the primary raw material in the production of their energy. The three largest Polish coal mining firms ([Węglokoks](/wiki/W%C4%99glokoks "Węglokoks"), [Kompania Węglowa](/wiki/Kompania_W%C4%99glowa "Kompania Węglowa") and [JSW](/wiki/Jastrz%C4%99bska_Sp%C3%B3%C5%82ka_W%C4%99glowa "Jastrzębska Spółka Węglowa")) extract around 100 million tonnes of coal annually.[[301]](#cite_note-305) After coal, Polish energy supply relies significantly on oil—the nation is the third-largest buyer of Russian oil exports to the EU.[[302]](#cite_note-306)

The new [Energy Policy of Poland until 2040](/wiki/Energy_in_Poland "Energy in Poland") (EPP2040) would reduce the share of coal and [lignite](/wiki/Lignite "Lignite") in electricity generation by 25% from 2017 to 2030. The plan involves deploying new nuclear plants, increasing energy efficiency, and decarbonising the Polish transport system in order to reduce greenhouse gas emissions and prioritise long-term energy security.[[300]](#cite_note-International_Energy_Agency_2022-304)[[303]](#cite_note-307)

### Science and technology

Main articles: [Timeline of Polish science and technology](/wiki/Timeline_of_Polish_science_and_technology "Timeline of Polish science and technology") and [List of Polish Nobel laureates](/wiki/List_of_Polish_Nobel_laureates "List of Polish Nobel laureates")

[![Marie Curie](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c8/Marie_Curie_c._1920s.jpg/250px-Marie_Curie_c._1920s.jpg)](/wiki/File:Marie_Curie_c._1920s.jpg)

Physicist and chemist [Maria Skłodowska-Curie](/wiki/Maria_Sk%C5%82odowska-Curie "Maria Skłodowska-Curie") was the first person to win two [Nobel Prizes](/wiki/Nobel_Prizes "Nobel Prizes").[[304]](#cite_note-Mould_1993-308)

[![Nicolaus Copernicus](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/Nikolaus_Kopernikus_MOT.jpg/250px-Nikolaus_Kopernikus_MOT.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nikolaus_Kopernikus_MOT.jpg)

Astronomer [Nicolaus Copernicus](/wiki/Nicolaus_Copernicus "Nicolaus Copernicus") formulated the [heliocentric](/wiki/Heliocentrism "Heliocentrism") model of the [Solar System](/wiki/Solar_System "Solar System").

Over the course of history, the Polish people have made considerable contributions in the fields of science, technology and mathematics.[[305]](#cite_note-309) Perhaps the most renowned Pole to support this theory was [Nicolaus Copernicus](/wiki/Nicolaus_Copernicus "Nicolaus Copernicus"), who triggered the [Copernican Revolution](/wiki/Copernican_Revolution "Copernican Revolution") by placing the [Sun rather than the Earth at the centre of the universe](/wiki/Heliocentrism "Heliocentrism").[[306]](#cite_note-310) He also derived a [quantity theory of money](/wiki/Quantity_theory_of_money "Quantity theory of money"), which made him a pioneer of economics. Copernicus' achievements and discoveries are considered the basis of Polish culture and cultural identity.[[307]](#cite_note-311) Poland was ranked 39th in the [Global Innovation Index](/wiki/Global_Innovation_Index "Global Innovation Index") in 2025.[[308]](#cite_note-312)[[309]](#cite_note-313)

Poland's tertiary education institutions; traditional [universities](/wiki/List_of_universities_in_Poland "List of universities in Poland"), as well as technical, medical, and economic institutions, employ around tens of thousands of researchers and staff members. There are hundreds of research and development institutes.[[310]](#cite_note-314) However, in the 19th and 20th centuries many Polish scientists worked abroad; one of the most important of these exiles was [Maria Skłodowska-Curie](/wiki/Maria_Sk%C5%82odowska-Curie "Maria Skłodowska-Curie"), a physicist and chemist who lived much of her life in France. In 1925, she established Poland's [Radium Institute](/wiki/Curie_Institute_(Warsaw) "Curie Institute (Warsaw)").[[304]](#cite_note-Mould_1993-308)

In the first half of the 20th century, Poland was a flourishing centre of mathematics. Outstanding Polish mathematicians formed the [Lwów School of Mathematics](/wiki/Lw%C3%B3w_School_of_Mathematics "Lwów School of Mathematics") (with [Stefan Banach](/wiki/Stefan_Banach "Stefan Banach"), [Stanisław Mazur](/wiki/Stanis%C5%82aw_Mazur "Stanisław Mazur"), [Hugo Steinhaus](/wiki/Hugo_Steinhaus "Hugo Steinhaus"), [Stanisław Ulam](/wiki/Stanis%C5%82aw_Ulam "Stanisław Ulam")) and [Warsaw School of Mathematics](/wiki/Warsaw_School_of_Mathematics "Warsaw School of Mathematics") (with [Alfred Tarski](/wiki/Alfred_Tarski "Alfred Tarski"), [Kazimierz Kuratowski](/wiki/Kazimierz_Kuratowski "Kazimierz Kuratowski"), [Wacław Sierpiński](/wiki/Wac%C5%82aw_Sierpi%C5%84ski "Wacław Sierpiński") and [Antoni Zygmund](/wiki/Antoni_Zygmund "Antoni Zygmund")). Numerous mathematicians, scientists, chemists or economists emigrated due to historic vicissitudes, among them [Benoit Mandelbrot](/wiki/Benoit_Mandelbrot "Benoit Mandelbrot"), [Leonid Hurwicz](/wiki/Leonid_Hurwicz "Leonid Hurwicz"), [Alfred Tarski](/wiki/Alfred_Tarski "Alfred Tarski"), [Joseph Rotblat](/wiki/Joseph_Rotblat "Joseph Rotblat") and Nobel Prize laureates [Roald Hoffmann](/wiki/Roald_Hoffmann "Roald Hoffmann"), [Georges Charpak](/wiki/Georges_Charpak "Georges Charpak") and [Tadeusz Reichstein](/wiki/Tadeusz_Reichstein "Tadeusz Reichstein").

## Demographics

Main articles: [Demographics of Poland](/wiki/Demographics_of_Poland "Demographics of Poland"), [List of cities and towns in Poland](/wiki/List_of_cities_and_towns_in_Poland "List of cities and towns in Poland"), [Metropolitan areas in Poland](/wiki/Metropolitan_areas_in_Poland "Metropolitan areas in Poland"), [Polish people](/wiki/Polish_people "Polish people"), and [Polish diaspora](/wiki/Polish_diaspora "Polish diaspora")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5f/Plac_Narutowicza_Warsaw_2023_skyline_aerial_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Plac_Narutowicza_Warsaw_2023_skyline_aerial_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Plac_Narutowicza_Warsaw_2023_skyline_aerial_(cropped).jpg)

[Warsaw](/wiki/Warsaw "Warsaw") is the largest [city in Poland](/wiki/List_of_cities_and_towns_in_Poland "List of cities and towns in Poland"), and an [economic hub of the country](/wiki/Economy_of_Poland "Economy of Poland").

Poland has a population of approximately 38.2 million as of 2021, and is the [ninth-most populous country](/wiki/List_of_European_countries_by_population "List of European countries by population") in Europe, as well as the fifth-most populous member state of the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union").[[311]](#cite_note-315) It has a population density of 122 inhabitants per square kilometre (320 inhabitants/mi2).[[312]](#cite_note-316) The [total fertility rate](/wiki/Total_fertility_rate "Total fertility rate") was estimated at 1.2 children born to a woman in 2023, which is [among the world's lowest](/wiki/List_of_sovereign_states_and_dependencies_by_total_fertility_rate "List of sovereign states and dependencies by total fertility rate").[[313]](#cite_note-317) Furthermore, Poland's population is [aging significantly](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_median_age "List of countries by median age"), and the country has a [median age](/wiki/Median_age "Median age") of 42.2.[[314]](#cite_note-318)

Around 60% of the country's population lives in urban areas or major cities and 40% in rural zones.[[315]](#cite_note-319) In 2020, 50.2% of Poles resided in [detached dwellings](/wiki/Detached_dwelling "Detached dwelling") and 44.3% in apartments.[[316]](#cite_note-320) The most populous administrative province or state is the [Masovian Voivodeship](/wiki/Masovian_Voivodeship "Masovian Voivodeship") and the most populous city is the capital, [Warsaw](/wiki/Warsaw "Warsaw"), at 1.8 million inhabitants with a further 2–3 million people living in its [metropolitan area](/wiki/Warsaw_metropolitan_area "Warsaw metropolitan area").[[317]](#cite_note-321)[[318]](#cite_note-322)[[319]](#cite_note-323) The [metropolitan area](/wiki/Katowice_urban_area "Katowice urban area") of [Katowice](/wiki/Katowice "Katowice") is the largest urban [conurbation](/wiki/Conurbation "Conurbation") with a population between 2.7 million[[320]](#cite_note-324) and 5.3 million residents.[[321]](#cite_note-325) Population density is higher in the south of Poland and mostly concentrated in [Upper Silesia](/wiki/Upper_Silesia "Upper Silesia"), between the cities of [Wrocław](/wiki/Wroc%C5%82aw "Wrocław") and [Kraków](/wiki/Krak%C3%B3w "Kraków").[[322]](#cite_note-326)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/2024-03_Katowice_archikatedra_taras_%285%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-2024-03_Katowice_archikatedra_taras_%285%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:2024-03_Katowice_archikatedra_taras_(5)_(cropped).jpg)

The [metropolitan area](/wiki/Katowice_urban_area "Katowice urban area") of [Katowice](/wiki/Katowice "Katowice") is the largest urban [conurbation](/wiki/Conurbation "Conurbation"), with [Upper Silesia](/wiki/Upper_Silesia "Upper Silesia") being the most densely populated region in Poland.

In the [2011 Polish census](/wiki/Polish_census_of_2011 "Polish census of 2011"), 37,310,341 people reported [Polish](/wiki/Polish_people "Polish people") identity, 846,719 [Silesian](/wiki/Silesians "Silesians"), 232,547 [Kashubian](/wiki/Kashubians "Kashubians") and 147,814 [German](/wiki/German_minority_in_Poland "German minority in Poland"). Other [identities](/wiki/Polish_people#National_minorities "Polish people") were reported by 163,363 people (0.41%) and 521,470 people (1.35%) did not specify any nationality.[[323]](#cite_note-Central_Statistical_Office_2015-327) Official population statistics do not include migrant workers who do not possess a permanent residency permit or [Karta Polaka](/wiki/Karta_Polaka "Karta Polaka").[[324]](#cite_note-328) More than 1.7 million [Ukrainian](/wiki/Ukrainians "Ukrainians") citizens worked legally in Poland in 2017.[[325]](#cite_note-329) The number of migrants is rising steadily; the country approved 504,172 work permits for foreigners in 2021 alone.[[326]](#cite_note-330)

According to the 2021 census, ethnic Poles comprise 98.84% of the population, including people who declared Polish heritage alone (96.28%) or both Polish and another ethnicity (2.56%) as responders were allowed to select up to two ethnicities. People who declared only non-Polish ethnicities made up 1.13% of the population and people who did not report their ethnicity numbered 0.03%. The province with the highest percentage of ethnic Poles was the [Holy Cross Voivodeship](/wiki/Holy_Cross_Voivodeship "Holy Cross Voivodeship") (99.70%), and the region with the lowest share of ethnic Poles was the [Silesian Voivodeship](/wiki/Silesian_Voivodeship "Silesian Voivodeship") (95.49%).[[327]](#cite_note-331)
