### Languages

Main articles: [Polish language](/wiki/Polish_language "Polish language"), [Languages of Poland](/wiki/Languages_of_Poland "Languages of Poland"), and [Bilingual communes in Poland](/wiki/Bilingual_communes_in_Poland "Bilingual communes in Poland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/Dolina_Jadwigi_znak.jpg/250px-Dolina_Jadwigi_znak.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dolina_Jadwigi_znak.jpg)

*[Dolina Jadwigi](/wiki/Dolina_Jadwigi "Dolina Jadwigi")*—a [bilingual](/wiki/Bilingual "Bilingual") Polish-[Kashubian](/wiki/Kashubian_language "Kashubian language") road sign with the village name

[Polish](/wiki/Polish_language "Polish language") is the [official](/wiki/Official_language "Official language") and predominant spoken language in Poland, and is one of the official [languages of the European Union](/wiki/Languages_of_the_European_Union "Languages of the European Union").[[329]](#cite_note-333) It is also a [second language](/wiki/Second_language "Second language") in parts of neighbouring [Lithuania](/wiki/Lithuania "Lithuania"), where it is taught in Polish-minority schools.[[330]](#cite_note-334)[[331]](#cite_note-335) Contemporary Poland is a linguistically [homogeneous](/wiki/Homogeneous "Homogeneous") nation, with 97% of respondents declaring Polish as their mother tongue.[[332]](#cite_note-336) There are currently 15 minority languages in Poland,[[333]](#cite_note-337) including one recognised regional language, [Kashubian](/wiki/Kashubian_language "Kashubian language"), which is spoken by approximately 100,000 people on a daily basis in the northern regions of [Kashubia](/wiki/Kashubia "Kashubia") and [Pomerania](/wiki/Pomerania "Pomerania").[[334]](#cite_note-338) Poland also recognises [secondary administrative languages or auxiliary languages in bilingual municipalities](/wiki/Bilingual_communes_in_Poland "Bilingual communes in Poland"), where bilingual signs and placenames are commonplace.[[335]](#cite_note-339) According to the [Centre for Public Opinion Research](/wiki/Centre_for_Public_Opinion_Research "Centre for Public Opinion Research"), around 32% of Polish citizens declared knowledge of the English language in 2015.[[336]](#cite_note-340)

### Religion

Main article: [Religion in Poland](/wiki/Religion_in_Poland "Religion in Poland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Pope_John_Paul_II_in_Krak%C3%B3w_%E2%80%93_1983.jpg/250px-Pope_John_Paul_II_in_Krak%C3%B3w_%E2%80%93_1983.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pope_John_Paul_II_in_Krak%C3%B3w_%E2%80%93_1983.jpg)

[John Paul II](/wiki/Pope_John_Paul_II "Pope John Paul II"), born Karol Wojtyła, held the papacy between 1978 and 2005 and was the first [Pole](/wiki/Polish_people "Polish people") to become [Pope](/wiki/Pope "Pope").

According to the 2021 census, 71.3% of all Polish citizens adhere to the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church_in_Poland "Catholic Church in Poland"), with 6.9% identifying as having no religion and 20.6% refusing to answer.[[3]](#cite_note-Census_2021-4)

Poland is one of the [most religious countries in Europe](/wiki/Religion_in_Europe "Religion in Europe"), where Catholicism remains a part of national identity and Polish-born [Pope John Paul II](/wiki/Pope_John_Paul_II "Pope John Paul II") is widely revered.[[337]](#cite_note-Rocca_2022-341)[[338]](#cite_note-342) In 2015, 61.6% of respondents outlined that religion is of high or very high importance.[[339]](#cite_note-343) However, church attendance has greatly decreased in recent years; only 28% of Catholics attended [mass](/wiki/Mass_(liturgy) "Mass (liturgy)") weekly in 2021, down from around half in 2000.[[340]](#cite_note-344) According to *[The Wall Street Journal](/wiki/The_Wall_Street_Journal "The Wall Street Journal")*, "Of [the] more than 100 countries studied by the *[Pew Research Center](/wiki/Pew_Research_Center "Pew Research Center")* in 2018, Poland was [secularising](/wiki/Secularization "Secularization") the fastest, as measured by the disparity between the religiosity of young people and their elders."[[337]](#cite_note-Rocca_2022-341)

Freedom of religion in Poland is guaranteed by the Constitution, and Poland's [concordat](/wiki/Concordat "Concordat") with the [Holy See](/wiki/Holy_See "Holy See") enables the teaching of religion in public schools.[[341]](#cite_note-345) Historically, the Polish state maintained a high degree of [religious tolerance](/wiki/Warsaw_Confederation "Warsaw Confederation") and provided asylum for refugees fleeing religious persecution in other parts of Europe.[[342]](#cite_note-346) Poland hosted Europe's largest [Jewish diaspora](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Poland "History of the Jews in Poland"), and the country was a centre of [Ashkenazi Jewish](/wiki/Ashkenazi_Jewish "Ashkenazi Jewish") culture and traditional learning until the [Holocaust](/wiki/The_Holocaust "The Holocaust").[[343]](#cite_note-347)

Contemporary religious minorities include [Eastern Orthodox Church](/wiki/Polish_Orthodox_Church "Polish Orthodox Church"), [Protestants](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism"), including [Lutherans](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism") of the [Evangelical-Augsburg Church](/wiki/Evangelical-Augsburg_Church_in_Poland "Evangelical-Augsburg Church in Poland"), [Pentecostals](/wiki/Pentecostalism "Pentecostalism") in the [Pentecostal Church in Poland](/wiki/Pentecostal_Church_in_Poland "Pentecostal Church in Poland"), [Adventists](/wiki/Adventists "Adventists") in the [Seventh-day Adventist Church](/wiki/Seventh-day_Adventist_Church "Seventh-day Adventist Church"). Other smaller Christian denominations include [Eastern Catholics](/wiki/Eastern_Catholic_Churches "Eastern Catholic Churches"), [Mariavites](/wiki/Mariavite_Church "Mariavite Church"), [Evangelicalism](/wiki/Evangelicalism "Evangelicalism") denominations. Other religions include [Jehovah's Witnesses](/wiki/Jehovah%27s_Witnesses "Jehovah's Witnesses"), [Jews](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Poland "History of the Jews in Poland"), [Muslims](/wiki/Islam_in_Poland "Islam in Poland") ([Tatars](/wiki/Tatars "Tatars")), and [neopagans](/wiki/Modern_Paganism "Modern Paganism"), some of whom are members of the [Native Polish Church](/wiki/Native_Polish_Church "Native Polish Church").[[344]](#cite_note-Central_Statistical_Office_2008-348)

### Health

Main article: [Health in Poland](/wiki/Health_in_Poland "Health in Poland")

Medical service providers and [hospitals](/wiki/Hospitals "Hospitals") in Poland are subordinate to the [Ministry of Health](/wiki/Ministry_of_Health_of_the_Republic_of_Poland "Ministry of Health of the Republic of Poland"); it provides administrative oversight and scrutiny of general medical practice and is obliged to maintain a high standard of [hygiene](/wiki/Hygiene "Hygiene") and patient care. Poland has a [universal healthcare system](/wiki/Universal_health_care "Universal health care") based on an all-inclusive [insurance system](/wiki/Health_insurance "Health insurance"); state subsidised healthcare is available to all citizens covered by the general health insurance programme of the [National Health Fund](/wiki/National_Health_Fund "National Health Fund") (NFZ). Private medical complexes exist nationwide; over 50% of the population uses both public and private sectors.[[345]](#cite_note-349)[[346]](#cite_note-350)[[347]](#cite_note-justlanded-351)

According to the [Human Development Report](/wiki/HDI "HDI") from 2020, the average life expectancy at birth is 79 years (around 75 years for an infant male and 83 years for an infant female);[[229]](#cite_note-:0-233) the country has a low [infant mortality rate](/wiki/Infant_mortality_rate "Infant mortality rate") (4 per 1,000 births).[[348]](#cite_note-352) In 2019, the principal cause of death was [ischemic heart disease](/wiki/Ischemic_heart_disease "Ischemic heart disease"); diseases of the [circulatory system](/wiki/Circulatory_system "Circulatory system") accounted for 45% of all deaths.[[349]](#cite_note-353) In the same year, Poland was also the 15th-largest importer of [medications](/wiki/Medications "Medications") and pharmaceutical products.[[350]](#cite_note-354)

### Education

Main articles: [Education in Poland](/wiki/Education_in_Poland "Education in Poland") and [Universities in Poland](/wiki/Universities_in_Poland "Universities in Poland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dd/Jagiellonian_University_Collegium_Maius%2C_courtyard%2C_15_Jagiello%C5%84ska_Street%2C_Old_Town%2C_Krak%C3%B3w%2C_Poland.jpg/250px-Jagiellonian_University_Collegium_Maius%2C_courtyard%2C_15_Jagiello%C5%84ska_Street%2C_Old_Town%2C_Krak%C3%B3w%2C_Poland.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jagiellonian_University_Collegium_Maius,_courtyard,_15_Jagiello%C5%84ska_Street,_Old_Town,_Krak%C3%B3w,_Poland.jpg)

[Jagiellonian University](/wiki/Jagiellonian_University "Jagiellonian University") in [Kraków](/wiki/Krak%C3%B3w "Kraków"), one of the world's oldest institutions of higher learning

The [Jagiellonian University](/wiki/Jagiellonian_University "Jagiellonian University") founded in 1364 by [Casimir III](/wiki/Casimir_III_the_Great "Casimir III the Great") in [Kraków](/wiki/Krak%C3%B3w "Kraków") was the first institution of higher learning established in Poland, and is one of the [oldest universities](/wiki/List_of_oldest_universities_in_continuous_operation "List of oldest universities in continuous operation") still in continuous operation.[[351]](#cite_note-355) Poland's [Commission of National Education](/wiki/Commission_of_National_Education "Commission of National Education") (*Komisja Edukacji Narodowej*), established in 1773, was the world's first state ministry of education.[[352]](#cite_note-356)[[353]](#cite_note-357) In 2018, the [Programme for International Student Assessment](/wiki/Programme_for_International_Student_Assessment "Programme for International Student Assessment"), coordinated by the [Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development](/wiki/Organisation_for_Economic_Co-operation_and_Development "Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development"), placed Poland's educational output as one of the highest in the OECD, ranking 5th by student attainment and 6th by student performance in 2022.[[354]](#cite_note-358)[[355]](#cite_note-359)

The framework for primary, secondary and higher tertiary education are established by the [Ministry of Education and Science](/wiki/Ministry_of_National_Education_(Poland) "Ministry of National Education (Poland)"). One year of kindergarten is [compulsory](/wiki/Compulsory_education "Compulsory education") for six-year-olds.[[356]](#cite_note-360)[[357]](#cite_note-Sejm_2016-361) Primary education traditionally begins at the age of seven, although children aged six can attend at the request of their parents or guardians.[[357]](#cite_note-Sejm_2016-361) Elementary school spans eight grades and secondary schooling is dependent on student preference – a four-year high school (*[liceum](/wiki/General_education_liceum "General education liceum")*), a five-year technical school (*[technikum](/wiki/Technikum_(Polish_education) "Technikum (Polish education)")*) or various [vocational studies](/wiki/Vocational_school "Vocational school") (*szkoła branżowa*) can be pursued by individual pupils.[[357]](#cite_note-Sejm_2016-361) A liceum or technikum is concluded with a maturity exit exam (*[matura](/wiki/Matura "Matura")*), which must be passed in order to apply for a university or other institutions of higher learning.[[358]](#cite_note-362)

In Poland, there are over 500 university-level institutions,[[359]](#cite_note-363) with numerous faculties.[[360]](#cite_note-364) The [University of Warsaw](/wiki/University_of_Warsaw "University of Warsaw") and [Warsaw Polytechnic](/wiki/Warsaw_Polytechnic "Warsaw Polytechnic"), the [University of Wrocław](/wiki/University_of_Wroc%C5%82aw "University of Wrocław"), [Adam Mickiewicz University in Poznań](/wiki/Adam_Mickiewicz_University_in_Pozna%C5%84 "Adam Mickiewicz University in Poznań") and the [University of Technology in Gdańsk](/wiki/Gda%C5%84sk_University_of_Technology "Gdańsk University of Technology") are among the most prominent.[[361]](#cite_note-365) There are three conventional [academic degrees](/wiki/Academic_degree "Academic degree") in Poland – *[licencjat](/wiki/Licentiate_(degree) "Licentiate (degree)")* or *[inżynier](/wiki/Bachelor%27s_degree "Bachelor's degree")* (first cycle), *[magister](/wiki/Magister_(degree) "Magister (degree)")* (second cycle) and *[doktor](/wiki/PhD "PhD")* (third cycle qualification).[[362]](#cite_note-OECD_2009-366)

## Culture

Main article: [Culture of Poland](/wiki/Culture_of_Poland "Culture of Poland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/Sigismund%27s_Chapel_01_AB.jpg/250px-Sigismund%27s_Chapel_01_AB.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sigismund%27s_Chapel_01_AB.jpg)

The [Polish White Eagle](/wiki/Coat_of_arms_of_Poland "Coat of arms of Poland") is Poland's enduring [national and cultural symbol](/wiki/National_symbols_of_Poland "National symbols of Poland").

The culture of Poland is closely connected with its intricate 1,000-year [history](/wiki/History_of_Poland "History of Poland"), and forms an important constituent in the [Western civilisation](/wiki/Western_world "Western world").[[363]](#cite_note-367) The Poles take great pride in their national identity which is often associated with the colours white and red, and exuded by the expression *biało-czerwoni* ("whitereds").[[364]](#cite_note-368) National symbols, chiefly the crowned [white-tailed eagle](/wiki/White-tailed_eagle "White-tailed eagle"), are often visible on clothing, insignia and emblems.[[365]](#cite_note-369) The architectural monuments of great importance are protected by the [National Heritage Board of Poland](/wiki/National_Heritage_Board_of_Poland "National Heritage Board of Poland").[[366]](#cite_note-370) Over 100 of the country's most significant tangible wonders were enlisted onto the [Historic Monuments Register](/wiki/List_of_Historic_Monuments_(Poland) "List of Historic Monuments (Poland)"),[[367]](#cite_note-371) with further 17 being recognised by [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") as World Heritage Sites.[[368]](#cite_note-372)

### Holidays and traditions

See also: [Christmas in Poland](/wiki/Christmas_in_Poland "Christmas in Poland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/Celebraci%C3%B3n_de_Todos_los_Santos%2C_cementerio_de_la_Santa_Cruz%2C_Gniezno%2C_Polonia%2C_2017-11-01%2C_DD_10-12_HDR.jpg/250px-Celebraci%C3%B3n_de_Todos_los_Santos%2C_cementerio_de_la_Santa_Cruz%2C_Gniezno%2C_Polonia%2C_2017-11-01%2C_DD_10-12_HDR.jpg)](/wiki/File:Celebraci%C3%B3n_de_Todos_los_Santos,_cementerio_de_la_Santa_Cruz,_Gniezno,_Polonia,_2017-11-01,_DD_10-12_HDR.jpg)

[All Saints' Day](/wiki/All_Saints%27_Day "All Saints' Day") on 1 November is one of the most important public holidays in Poland.

There are 13 government-approved annual public holidays – New Year on 1 January, [Three Kings' Day](/wiki/Epiphany_(holiday) "Epiphany (holiday)") on 6 January, [Easter Sunday](/wiki/Easter_Sunday "Easter Sunday") and [Easter Monday](/wiki/Easter_Monday "Easter Monday"), [Labour Day](/wiki/International_Workers%27_Day "International Workers' Day") on 1 May, [Constitution Day](/wiki/3_May_Constitution_Day "3 May Constitution Day") on 3 May, [Pentecost](/wiki/Pentecost "Pentecost"), [Corpus Christi](/wiki/Feast_of_Corpus_Christi "Feast of Corpus Christi"), [Feast of the Assumption](/wiki/Assumption_of_Mary "Assumption of Mary") on 15 August, [All Saints' Day](/wiki/All_Saints%27_Day "All Saints' Day") on 1 November, [Independence Day](/wiki/Independence_Day_(Poland) "Independence Day (Poland)") on 11 November and Christmastide on 25 and 26 December.[[369]](#cite_note-373)

Particular traditions and superstitious customs observed in Poland are not found elsewhere in Europe. Though Christmas Eve (*[Wigilia](/wiki/Wigilia "Wigilia")*) is not a public holiday, it remains the most memorable day of the entire year. [Trees](/wiki/Christmas_trees "Christmas trees") are decorated on 24 December, hay is placed under the tablecloth to resemble Jesus' [manger](/wiki/Manger "Manger"), [Christmas wafers](/wiki/Christmas_wafer "Christmas wafer") (*opłatek*) are shared between gathered guests and a [twelve-dish meatless supper](/wiki/Twelve-dish_Christmas_Eve_supper "Twelve-dish Christmas Eve supper") is served that same evening when the [first star](/wiki/Star_of_Bethlehem "Star of Bethlehem") appears.[[370]](#cite_note-374) An empty plate and seat are symbolically left at the table for an unexpected guest.[[371]](#cite_note-375) On occasion, [carolers](/wiki/Christmas_carols "Christmas carols") journey around smaller towns with a folk [Turoń](/wiki/Turo%C5%84 "Turoń") creature until the [Lent](/wiki/Lent "Lent") period.[[372]](#cite_note-376)

A widely popular [doughnut](/wiki/P%C4%85czki "Pączki") and sweet pastry feast occurs on [Fat Thursday](/wiki/Fat_Thursday "Fat Thursday"), usually 52 days prior to Easter.[[373]](#cite_note-Borodo_2020-377) [Eggs](/wiki/Easter_egg "Easter egg") for [Holy Sunday](/wiki/Easter_Sunday "Easter Sunday") are painted and placed in decorated [baskets](/wiki/%C5%9Awi%C4%99conka "Święconka") that are previously blessed by clergymen in churches on [Easter Saturday](/wiki/Easter_Saturday "Easter Saturday"). Easter Monday is celebrated with pagan *[dyngus](/wiki/%C5%9Amigus-dyngus "Śmigus-dyngus")* festivities, where the youth is engaged in water fights.[[374]](#cite_note-378)[[373]](#cite_note-Borodo_2020-377) Cemeteries and graves of the deceased are annually visited by family members on All Saints' Day; tombstones are cleaned as a sign of respect and candles are lit to honour the dead on an unprecedented scale.[[375]](#cite_note-379)

### Music

Main article: [Music of Poland](/wiki/Music_of_Poland "Music of Poland")

[![Frédéric Chopin](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e8/Frederic_Chopin_photo.jpeg/250px-Frederic_Chopin_photo.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Frederic_Chopin_photo.jpeg)

[Frédéric Chopin](/wiki/Fr%C3%A9d%C3%A9ric_Chopin "Frédéric Chopin") was a renowned classical composer and virtuoso pianist.

[![Artur Rubinstein](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/Optreden_Arthur_Rubinstein_in_Concertgebouw%2C_Bestanddeelnr_913-5228.jpg/250px-Optreden_Arthur_Rubinstein_in_Concertgebouw%2C_Bestanddeelnr_913-5228.jpg)](/wiki/File:Optreden_Arthur_Rubinstein_in_Concertgebouw,_Bestanddeelnr_913-5228.jpg)

[Artur Rubinstein](/wiki/Arthur_Rubinstein "Arthur Rubinstein") was one of the greatest concert pianists of the 20th century.

Artists from Poland, including famous musicians such as [Frédéric Chopin](/wiki/Fr%C3%A9d%C3%A9ric_Chopin "Frédéric Chopin"), [Artur Rubinstein](/wiki/Artur_Rubinstein "Artur Rubinstein"), [Ignacy Jan Paderewski](/wiki/Ignacy_Jan_Paderewski "Ignacy Jan Paderewski"), [Krzysztof Penderecki](/wiki/Krzysztof_Penderecki "Krzysztof Penderecki"), [Henryk Wieniawski](/wiki/Henryk_Wieniawski "Henryk Wieniawski"), [Karol Szymanowski](/wiki/Karol_Szymanowski "Karol Szymanowski"), [Witold Lutosławski](/wiki/Witold_Lutos%C5%82awski "Witold Lutosławski"), [Stanisław Moniuszko](/wiki/Stanis%C5%82aw_Moniuszko "Stanisław Moniuszko") and traditional, regionalised [folk composers](/wiki/Folk_music "Folk music") create a lively and diverse music scene, which even recognises its own music genres, such as [sung poetry](/wiki/Sung_poetry "Sung poetry") and [disco polo](/wiki/Disco_polo "Disco polo").[[376]](#cite_note-380)

The origins of Polish music can be traced to the 13th century; manuscripts have been found in [Stary Sącz](/wiki/Stary_S%C4%85cz "Stary Sącz") containing [polyphonic](/wiki/Polyphony "Polyphony") compositions related to the Parisian [Notre Dame School](/wiki/Notre_Dame_School "Notre Dame School"). Other early compositions, such as the melody of *[Bogurodzica](/wiki/Bogurodzica "Bogurodzica")* and *[God Is Born](/wiki/God_Is_Born "God Is Born")* (a coronation [polonaise tune](/wiki/Polonaise_(dance) "Polonaise (dance)") for Polish kings by an unknown composer), may also date back to this period, however, the first known notable composer, [Nicholas of Radom](/wiki/Miko%C5%82aj_z_Radomia "Mikołaj z Radomia"), lived in the 15th century. [Diomedes Cato](/wiki/Diomedes_Cato "Diomedes Cato"), a native-born Italian who lived in Kraków, became a renowned lutenist at the court of Sigismund III; he not only imported some of the musical styles from southern Europe but blended them with native folk music.[[377]](#cite_note-The_Music_Courts_of_the_Polish_Vasas-381)

In the 17th and 18th centuries, Polish baroque composers wrote [liturgical music](/wiki/Liturgical_music "Liturgical music") and secular compositions such as concertos and [sonatas](/wiki/Sonata "Sonata") for voices or instruments. At the end of the 18th century, Polish classical music evolved into national forms like the [polonaise](/wiki/Polonaise "Polonaise"). [Wojciech Bogusławski](/wiki/Wojciech_Bogus%C5%82awski "Wojciech Bogusławski") is accredited with composing the first Polish national opera, titled *[Krakowiacy i Górale](https://pl.wikipedia.org/wiki/Krakowiacy_i_G%C3%B3rale "pl:Krakowiacy i Górale")*, which premiered in 1794.[[378]](#cite_note-382)

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Gnome-mime-sound-openclipart.svg/60px-Gnome-mime-sound-openclipart.svg.png)

**[Fryderyk Chopin](/wiki/Fryderyk_Chopin "Fryderyk Chopin")**

[Mazurka no. 4 in a minor, op. 17](/wiki/File:Frederic_Chopin_-_mazurka_no._4_in_a_minor,_op._17.ogg "File:Frederic Chopin - mazurka no. 4 in a minor, op. 17.ogg")

[Mazurka](/wiki/Mazurka "Mazurka") ([Polish](/wiki/Polish_language "Polish language"): *mazurek*), stylised [folk dance](/wiki/Folk_dance "Folk dance") in [triple meter](/wiki/Triple_meter "Triple meter") (1832), commemorating the [November Uprising](/wiki/November_Uprising "November Uprising")

Poland today has an active music scene, with the jazz and metal genres being particularly popular among the contemporary populace. Polish jazz musicians such as [Krzysztof Komeda](/wiki/Krzysztof_Komeda "Krzysztof Komeda") created a unique style, which was most famous in the 1960s and 1970s and continues to be popular to this day. Poland has also become a major venue for large-scale music festivals, chief among which are the [Pol'and'Rock Festival](/wiki/Pol%27and%27Rock_Festival "Pol'and'Rock Festival"),[[379]](#cite_note-383) [Open'er Festival](/wiki/Open%27er_Festival "Open'er Festival"), [Opole Festival](/wiki/Opole_Festival "Opole Festival") and [Sopot Festival](/wiki/Sopot_Festival "Sopot Festival").[[380]](#cite_note-384)

### Art

Main articles: [Art in Poland](/wiki/Art_in_Poland "Art in Poland") and [Young Poland](/wiki/Young_Poland "Young Poland")

[![Jan Matejko](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/Matejko_Self-portrait.jpg/250px-Matejko_Self-portrait.jpg)](/wiki/File:Matejko_Self-portrait.jpg)

[Jan Matejko](/wiki/Jan_Matejko "Jan Matejko"), leading Polish [history painter](/wiki/History_painting "History painting") whose works depict Poland's heritage and key historical events

[![Lady with an Ermine](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bf/Lady_with_an_Ermine_-_Leonardo_da_Vinci_%28adjusted_levels%29.jpg/250px-Lady_with_an_Ermine_-_Leonardo_da_Vinci_%28adjusted_levels%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lady_with_an_Ermine_-_Leonardo_da_Vinci_(adjusted_levels).jpg)

*[Lady with an Ermine](/wiki/Lady_with_an_Ermine "Lady with an Ermine")* (1490) by [Leonardo da Vinci](/wiki/Leonardo_da_Vinci "Leonardo da Vinci") is displayed in the [Czartoryski Museum](/wiki/Czartoryski_Museum "Czartoryski Museum") in Kraków.

Art in Poland has invariably reflected [European](/wiki/Art_of_Europe "Art of Europe") trends, with Polish painting pivoted on [folklore](/wiki/Folklore "Folklore"), [Catholic themes](/wiki/Catholic_art "Catholic art"), [historicism](/wiki/History_painting "History painting") and [realism](/wiki/Realism_(arts) "Realism (arts)"), but also on [Impressionism](/wiki/Impressionism "Impressionism") and [romanticism](/wiki/Romanticism_in_Poland "Romanticism in Poland"). An important art movement was [Young Poland](/wiki/Young_Poland "Young Poland"), developed in the late 19th century for promoting [decadence](/wiki/Decadent_movement "Decadent movement"), [symbolism](/wiki/Symbolism_(arts) "Symbolism (arts)") and [Art Nouveau](/wiki/Art_Nouveau "Art Nouveau"). Since the 20th century Polish documentary art and photography has enjoyed worldwide fame, especially the [Polish School of Posters](/wiki/Polish_School_of_Posters "Polish School of Posters").[[381]](#cite_note-MFA-385) One of the most distinguished paintings in Poland is *[Lady with an Ermine](/wiki/Lady_with_an_Ermine "Lady with an Ermine")* (1490) by [Leonardo da Vinci](/wiki/Leonardo_da_Vinci "Leonardo da Vinci").[[382]](#cite_note-386)

Internationally renowned Polish artists include [Jan Matejko](/wiki/Jan_Matejko "Jan Matejko") (historicism), [Jacek Malczewski](/wiki/Jacek_Malczewski "Jacek Malczewski") (symbolism), [Stanisław Wyspiański](/wiki/Stanis%C5%82aw_Wyspia%C5%84ski "Stanisław Wyspiański") (art nouveau), [Henryk Siemiradzki](/wiki/Henryk_Siemiradzki "Henryk Siemiradzki") (Roman [academic art](/wiki/Academic_art "Academic art")), [Tamara de Lempicka](/wiki/Tamara_de_Lempicka "Tamara de Lempicka") ([art deco](/wiki/Art_deco "Art deco")), and [Zdzisław Beksiński](/wiki/Zdzis%C5%82aw_Beksi%C5%84ski "Zdzisław Beksiński") (dystopian [surrealism](/wiki/Surrealism "Surrealism")).[[383]](#cite_note-387) Several Polish artists and sculptors were also acclaimed representatives of [avant-garde](/wiki/Avant-garde "Avant-garde"), [constructivist](/wiki/Constructivism_(art) "Constructivism (art)"), [minimalist](/wiki/Minimalism "Minimalism") and contemporary art movements, including [Katarzyna Kobro](/wiki/Katarzyna_Kobro "Katarzyna Kobro"), [Władysław Strzemiński](/wiki/W%C5%82adys%C5%82aw_Strzemi%C5%84ski "Władysław Strzemiński"), [Magdalena Abakanowicz](/wiki/Magdalena_Abakanowicz "Magdalena Abakanowicz"), [Alina Szapocznikow](/wiki/Alina_Szapocznikow "Alina Szapocznikow"), [Igor Mitoraj](/wiki/Igor_Mitoraj "Igor Mitoraj") and [Wilhelm Sasnal](/wiki/Wilhelm_Sasnal "Wilhelm Sasnal").

Notable art academies in Poland include the [Kraków Academy of Fine Arts](/wiki/Krak%C3%B3w_Academy_of_Fine_Arts "Kraków Academy of Fine Arts"), [Academy of Fine Arts in Warsaw](/wiki/Academy_of_Fine_Arts_in_Warsaw "Academy of Fine Arts in Warsaw"), [Art Academy of Szczecin](/wiki/Art_Academy_of_Szczecin "Art Academy of Szczecin"), [University of Fine Arts in Poznań](/wiki/University_of_Fine_Arts_in_Pozna%C5%84 "University of Fine Arts in Poznań") and the [Geppert Academy of Fine Arts in Wrocław](/wiki/Eugeniusz_Geppert_Academy_of_Fine_Arts "Eugeniusz Geppert Academy of Fine Arts"). Contemporary works are exhibited at [Zachęta](/wiki/Zach%C4%99ta "Zachęta"), [Ujazdów](/wiki/Ujazd%C3%B3w_Castle "Ujazdów Castle"), and [MOCAK](/wiki/MOCAK,_Museum_of_Contemporary_Art_in_Krak%C3%B3w "MOCAK, Museum of Contemporary Art in Kraków") art galleries.[[384]](#cite_note-388)

### Architecture

Main article: [Architecture of Poland](/wiki/Architecture_of_Poland "Architecture of Poland")

[![Saint Mary's Church in Kraków](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/St_Mary%27s_Krakow.jpg/250px-St_Mary%27s_Krakow.jpg)](/wiki/File:St_Mary%27s_Krakow.jpg)

[St. Mary's Basilica](/wiki/St._Mary%27s_Basilica,_Krak%C3%B3w "St. Mary's Basilica, Kraków") on the [Main Market Square](/wiki/Main_Market_Square,_Krak%C3%B3w "Main Market Square, Kraków") in [Kraków](/wiki/Krak%C3%B3w "Kraków") is an example of [Brick Gothic](/wiki/Brick_Gothic "Brick Gothic") architecture.

[![Poznań City Hall](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/62/Ayuntamiento%2C_Poznan%2C_Polonia%2C_2014-09-18%2C_DD_67-72_HDR.jpg/250px-Ayuntamiento%2C_Poznan%2C_Polonia%2C_2014-09-18%2C_DD_67-72_HDR.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ayuntamiento,_Poznan,_Polonia,_2014-09-18,_DD_67-72_HDR.jpg)

The 16th-century [City Hall](/wiki/Pozna%C5%84_Town_Hall "Poznań Town Hall") of [Poznań](/wiki/Pozna%C5%84 "Poznań") illustrates the [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance_in_Poland "Renaissance in Poland") style.

The [architecture of Poland](/wiki/Architecture_of_Poland "Architecture of Poland") reflects [European architectural](/wiki/Architecture_of_Europe "Architecture of Europe") styles, with strong historical influences derived from [Italy](/wiki/Architecture_of_Italy "Architecture of Italy"), [Germany](/wiki/Architecture_of_Germany "Architecture of Germany"), and the [Low Countries](/wiki/Low_Countries "Low Countries").[[385]](#cite_note-389) Settlements founded on [Magdeburg Law](/wiki/Magdeburg_rights "Magdeburg rights") evolved around [central marketplaces](/wiki/Town_square "Town square") (*plac*, *rynek*), encircled by a grid or [concentric](/wiki/Concentric_objects "Concentric objects") network of streets forming an [old town](/wiki/Old_town "Old town") (*stare miasto*).[[386]](#cite_note-390) Poland's traditional landscape is characterised by ornate churches, [city tenements](/wiki/Kamienica_(architecture) "Kamienica (architecture)") and [town halls](/wiki/Ratusz "Ratusz").[[387]](#cite_note-391) [Cloth hall markets](/wiki/Cloth_hall "Cloth hall") (*sukiennice*) were once an abundant feature of Polish urban architecture.[[388]](#cite_note-392) The mountainous south is known for its [Zakopane chalet style](/wiki/Zakopane_Style "Zakopane Style"), which originated in Poland.[[389]](#cite_note-393)

The earliest architectonic trend was [Romanesque](/wiki/Romanesque_architecture "Romanesque architecture") (c. 11th century), but its traces in the form of [circular rotundas](/wiki/Rotunda_(architecture) "Rotunda (architecture)") are scarce.[[390]](#cite_note-394) The arrival of [brick Gothic](/wiki/Brick_Gothic "Brick Gothic") (c. 13th century) defined Poland's most distinguishable medieval style, exuded by the castles of [Malbork](/wiki/Malbork_Castle "Malbork Castle"), [Lidzbark](/wiki/Lidzbark_Castle "Lidzbark Castle"), [Gniew](/wiki/Gniew_Castle "Gniew Castle") and [Kwidzyn](/wiki/Kwidzyn_Castle "Kwidzyn Castle") as well as the cathedrals of [Gniezno](/wiki/Gniezno_Cathedral "Gniezno Cathedral"), [Gdańsk](/wiki/St._Mary%27s_Church,_Gda%C5%84sk "St. Mary's Church, Gdańsk"), [Wrocław](/wiki/Wroc%C5%82aw_Cathedral "Wrocław Cathedral"), [Frombork](/wiki/Frombork_Cathedral "Frombork Cathedral") and [Kraków](/wiki/St._Mary%27s_Basilica,_Krak%C3%B3w "St. Mary's Basilica, Kraków").[[391]](#cite_note-395) The [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance_in_Poland "Renaissance in Poland") (16th century) gave rise to Italianate courtyards, defensive [palazzos](/wiki/Palazzo "Palazzo") and [mausoleums](/wiki/Sigismund%27s_Chapel "Sigismund's Chapel").[[392]](#cite_note-396) Decorative [attics](/wiki/Attic_(architecture) "Attic (architecture)") with [pinnacles](/wiki/Pinnacle "Pinnacle") and [arcade](/wiki/Arcade_(architecture) "Arcade (architecture)") [loggias](/wiki/Loggia "Loggia") are elements of [Polish Mannerism](/wiki/Mannerist_architecture "Mannerist architecture"), found in [Poznań](/wiki/Pozna%C5%84 "Poznań"), [Lublin](/wiki/Lublin "Lublin") and [Zamość](/wiki/Zamo%C5%9B%C4%87 "Zamość").[[393]](#cite_note-397)[[394]](#cite_note-Mikos-398) Foreign artisans often came at the expense of kings or nobles, whose palaces were built thereafter in the [Baroque](/wiki/Baroque_in_Poland "Baroque in Poland"), [Neoclassical](/wiki/Neoclassical_architecture_in_Poland "Neoclassical architecture in Poland") and [Revivalist](/wiki/Revivalism_(architecture) "Revivalism (architecture)") styles (17th–19th century).[[395]](#cite_note-399)

Primary building materials [timber](/wiki/Lumber "Lumber") and [red brick](/wiki/Brick "Brick") were used extensively in Polish folk architecture,[[396]](#cite_note-400) and the concept of a [fortified church](/wiki/Fortified_church "Fortified church") was commonplace.[[397]](#cite_note-401) Secular structures such as [*dworek* manor houses](/wiki/Dw%C3%B3r_(manor_house) "Dwór (manor house)"), [farmsteads](/wiki/Farmhouses "Farmhouses"), [granaries](/wiki/Granary "Granary"), [mills](/wiki/Gristmill "Gristmill") and country [inns](/wiki/Inn "Inn") are still present in some regions or in open air museums (*[skansen](/wiki/Open-Air_Museum "Open-Air Museum")*).[[398]](#cite_note-402) However, traditional construction methods faded in the early-mid 20th century due to urbanisation and the construction of [functionalist](/wiki/Functionalist_architecture "Functionalist architecture") [housing estates](/wiki/Housing_estate "Housing estate") and [residential areas](/wiki/Residential_architecture_in_Poland "Residential architecture in Poland").[[399]](#cite_note-403)

### Literature

Main articles: [Polish literature](/wiki/Polish_literature "Polish literature") and [History of philosophy in Poland](/wiki/History_of_philosophy_in_Poland "History of philosophy in Poland")

[![Adam Mickiewicz](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/10/Wa%C5%84kowicz_Adam_Mickiewicz.jpg/250px-Wa%C5%84kowicz_Adam_Mickiewicz.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wa%C5%84kowicz_Adam_Mickiewicz.jpg)

[Adam Mickiewicz](/wiki/Adam_Mickiewicz "Adam Mickiewicz"), whose [national epic poem](/wiki/Epic_poetry "Epic poetry") *[Pan Tadeusz](/wiki/Pan_Tadeusz "Pan Tadeusz")* (1834) is considered a masterpiece of [Polish literature](/wiki/Polish_literature "Polish literature")

[![Joseph Conrad-Korzeniowski](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/Joseph_Conrad%2C_Fotografie_von_George_Charles_Beresford%2C_1904.jpg/250px-Joseph_Conrad%2C_Fotografie_von_George_Charles_Beresford%2C_1904.jpg)](/wiki/File:Joseph_Conrad,_Fotografie_von_George_Charles_Beresford,_1904.jpg)

[Joseph Conrad](/wiki/Joseph_Conrad "Joseph Conrad"), author of popular books such as *[Heart of Darkness](/wiki/Heart_of_Darkness "Heart of Darkness")* (1899) and *[Nostromo](/wiki/Nostromo "Nostromo")* (1904)

The [literary works of Poland](/wiki/Polish_literature "Polish literature") have traditionally concentrated around the themes of patriotism, [spirituality](/wiki/Spirituality "Spirituality"), social [allegories](/wiki/Allegory "Allegory") and moral narratives.[[400]](#cite_note-404) The earliest examples of Polish literature, written in [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin"), date to the 12th century.[[401]](#cite_note-Koca_2006-405) The first [Polish](/wiki/Polish_language "Polish language") phrase *Day ut ia pobrusa, a ti poziwai* (officially translated as "Let me, I shall grind, and you take a rest") was documented in the [Book of Henryków](/wiki/Book_of_Henryk%C3%B3w "Book of Henryków") and reflected the use of a [quern-stone](/wiki/Quern-stone "Quern-stone").[[402]](#cite_note-406) It has been since included in [UNESCO's Memory of World Register](/wiki/Memory_of_the_World_Programme "Memory of the World Programme").[[403]](#cite_note-407) The oldest extant manuscripts of fine [prose](/wiki/Prose "Prose") in [Old Polish](/wiki/Old_Polish_language "Old Polish language") are the [Holy Cross Sermons](/wiki/Holy_Cross_Sermons "Holy Cross Sermons") and the [Bible of Queen Sophia](/wiki/Bible_of_Queen_Sophia "Bible of Queen Sophia"),[[404]](#cite_note-408) and [Calendarium cracoviense](/wiki/Almanach_cracoviense_ad_annum_1474 "Almanach cracoviense ad annum 1474") (1474) is Poland's oldest surviving [print](/wiki/Printing_press "Printing press").[[405]](#cite_note-409)

The poets [Jan Kochanowski](/wiki/Jan_Kochanowski "Jan Kochanowski") and [Nicholas Rey](/wiki/Nicholas_Rey "Nicholas Rey") became the first [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance_in_Poland "Renaissance in Poland") authors to write in Polish.[[406]](#cite_note-410) Prime literarians of the period included [Dantiscus](/wiki/Johannes_Dantiscus "Johannes Dantiscus"), [Modrevius](/wiki/Andreus_Fricius_Modrevius "Andreus Fricius Modrevius"), [Goslicius](/wiki/Wawrzyniec_Go%C5%9Blicki "Wawrzyniec Goślicki"), [Sarbievius](/wiki/Matthias_Sarbievius "Matthias Sarbievius") and theologian [John Laski](/wiki/John_Laski "John Laski"). In the [Baroque](/wiki/Polish_Baroque "Polish Baroque") era, [Jesuit philosophy](/wiki/Jesuits "Jesuits") and local culture greatly influenced the literary techniques of [Jan Andrzej Morsztyn](/wiki/Jan_Andrzej_Morsztyn "Jan Andrzej Morsztyn") ([Marinism](/wiki/Marinism "Marinism")) and [Jan Chryzostom Pasek](/wiki/Jan_Chryzostom_Pasek "Jan Chryzostom Pasek") ([sarmatian](/wiki/Sarmatism "Sarmatism") memoirs).[[407]](#cite_note-411) During the [Enlightenment](/wiki/Polish_Enlightenment "Polish Enlightenment"), playwright [Ignacy Krasicki](/wiki/Ignacy_Krasicki "Ignacy Krasicki") composed the first Polish-language [novel](/wiki/The_Adventures_of_Mr._Nicholas_Wisdom "The Adventures of Mr. Nicholas Wisdom").[[408]](#cite_note-412) Poland's leading 19th-century [romantic poets](/wiki/Romanticism_in_Poland "Romanticism in Poland") were the [Three Bards](/wiki/Three_Bards "Three Bards") – [Juliusz Słowacki](/wiki/Juliusz_S%C5%82owacki "Juliusz Słowacki"), [Zygmunt Krasiński](/wiki/Zygmunt_Krasi%C5%84ski "Zygmunt Krasiński") and [Adam Mickiewicz](/wiki/Adam_Mickiewicz "Adam Mickiewicz"), whose epic poem *[Pan Tadeusz](/wiki/Pan_Tadeusz "Pan Tadeusz")* (1834) is a national classic.[[409]](#cite_note-413) In the 20th century, the English [impressionist](/wiki/Impressionism_(literature) "Impressionism (literature)") and early [modernist](/wiki/Literary_modernism "Literary modernism") writings of [Joseph Conrad](/wiki/Joseph_Conrad "Joseph Conrad") made him one of the most eminent novelists of all time.[[410]](#cite_note-414)[[411]](#cite_note-415)

Contemporary Polish literature is versatile, with its fantasy genre having been particularly praised.[[412]](#cite_note-416) The philosophical [sci-fi](/wiki/Science-fiction "Science-fiction") novel *[Solaris](/wiki/Solaris_(novel) "Solaris (novel)")* by [Stanisław Lem](/wiki/Stanis%C5%82aw_Lem "Stanisław Lem") and *[The Witcher](/wiki/The_Witcher "The Witcher")* series by [Andrzej Sapkowski](/wiki/Andrzej_Sapkowski "Andrzej Sapkowski") are celebrated works of world fiction.[[413]](#cite_note-417) Poland has six [Nobel-Prize winning](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Literature "Nobel Prize in Literature") authors – [Henryk Sienkiewicz](/wiki/Henryk_Sienkiewicz "Henryk Sienkiewicz") (*[Quo Vadis](/wiki/Quo_Vadis_(novel) "Quo Vadis (novel)")*; 1905), [Władysław Reymont](/wiki/W%C5%82adys%C5%82aw_Reymont "Władysław Reymont") (*[The Peasants](/wiki/The_Peasants "The Peasants")*; 1924), [Isaac Bashevis Singer](/wiki/Isaac_Bashevis_Singer "Isaac Bashevis Singer") (1978), [Czesław Miłosz](/wiki/Czes%C5%82aw_Mi%C5%82osz "Czesław Miłosz") (1980), [Wisława Szymborska](/wiki/Wis%C5%82awa_Szymborska "Wisława Szymborska") (1996), and [Olga Tokarczuk](/wiki/Olga_Tokarczuk "Olga Tokarczuk") (2018).[[414]](#cite_note-Facts_on_the_Nobel_Prize_in_Literature-418)[[415]](#cite_note-Gopnik_2007-419)[[416]](#cite_note-420)

### Cuisine

Main article: [Polish cuisine](/wiki/Polish_cuisine "Polish cuisine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/Healthy-regional-dishes-1329449.jpg/250px-Healthy-regional-dishes-1329449.jpg)](/wiki/File:Healthy-regional-dishes-1329449.jpg)

[Kielbasa](/wiki/Kielbasa "Kielbasa") sausage, a staple of Polish cuisine, with [pickled cucumbers](/wiki/Pickled_cucumber "Pickled cucumber") and [rye bread](/wiki/Rye_bread "Rye bread")

The cuisine of Poland is eclectic, with many regional varieties, sharing some similarity with other neighbouring cuisines.[[417]](#cite_note-Puri-421) Among the staple or regional dishes are [pierogi](/wiki/Pierogi "Pierogi") (filled dumplings), [kielbasa](/wiki/Kielbasa "Kielbasa") (sausage), [bigos](/wiki/Bigos "Bigos") (hunter's stew), [kotlet schabowy](/wiki/Kotlet_schabowy "Kotlet schabowy") (breaded cutlet), [gołąbki](/wiki/Go%C5%82%C4%85bki "Gołąbki") (cabbage rolls), [barszcz](/wiki/Borscht "Borscht") (borscht), [żurek](/wiki/%C5%BBurek "Żurek") (soured rye soup), [oscypek](/wiki/Oscypek "Oscypek") (smoked cheese), and [tomato soup](/wiki/Tomato_soup "Tomato soup").[[418]](#cite_note-Marc_Heine-422)[[419]](#cite_note-Strybel_2003-423) [Bagels](/wiki/Bagel "Bagel"), a type of [bread roll](/wiki/Bread_roll "Bread roll"), also originated in Poland.[[420]](#cite_note-424)

Traditional dishes are hearty and abundant in pork, potatoes, eggs, cream, mushrooms, regional herbs, and sauce.[[421]](#cite_note-425) Polish food is characteristic for its various kinds of [kluski](/wiki/Kluski "Kluski") (soft dumplings), [soups](/wiki/Soup "Soup"), cereals and a variety of breads and [open sandwiches](/wiki/Open_sandwich "Open sandwich"). Salads, including [mizeria](/wiki/Mizeria "Mizeria") (cucumber salad), [coleslaw](/wiki/Coleslaw "Coleslaw"), [sauerkraut](/wiki/Sauerkraut "Sauerkraut"), carrot and [seared beets](/wiki/Searing "Searing"), are common. Meals conclude with a dessert such as [sernik](/wiki/Cheesecake "Cheesecake") (cheesecake), [makowiec](/wiki/Makowiec_(pastry) "Makowiec (pastry)") (poppy seed roll), or [napoleonka](/wiki/Napoleonka "Napoleonka") ([mille-feuille](/wiki/Mille-feuille "Mille-feuille")) cream pie.[[422]](#cite_note-426)

Traditional alcoholic beverages include honey [mead](/wiki/Mead_in_Poland "Mead in Poland"), widespread since the 13th century, [beer](/wiki/Beer_in_Poland "Beer in Poland"), wine and [vodka](/wiki/Vodka "Vodka").[[423]](#cite_note-427) The world's first written mention of vodka originates from Poland.[[424]](#cite_note-428) The most popular alcoholic drinks at present are beer and wine which took over from vodka more popular in the years 1980–1998.[[425]](#cite_note-beverages-429) [Grodziskie](/wiki/Grodziskie "Grodziskie"), sometimes referred to as "Polish Champagne", is an example of a historical beer style from Poland.[[426]](#cite_note-430) Tea remains common in Polish society since the 19th century, whilst coffee is drunk widely since the 18th century.[[427]](#cite_note-431)

### Fashion and design

Further information: [Category:Polish fashion](/wiki/Category:Polish_fashion "Category:Polish fashion")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/Robe_%C3%A0_la_Polonaise_MET_DT11155.jpg/250px-Robe_%C3%A0_la_Polonaise_MET_DT11155.jpg)](/wiki/File:Robe_%C3%A0_la_Polonaise_MET_DT11155.jpg)

Traditional *[polonaise](/wiki/Polonaise_(clothing) "Polonaise (clothing)")* dresses, 1780–1785

Several Polish designers and stylists left a legacy of beauty inventions and cosmetics; including [Helena Rubinstein](/wiki/Helena_Rubinstein "Helena Rubinstein") and [Maksymilian Faktorowicz](/wiki/Maksymilian_Faktorowicz "Maksymilian Faktorowicz"), who created a line of cosmetics company in California known as [Max Factor](/wiki/Max_Factor "Max Factor") and formulated the term "make-up" which is now widely used as an alternative for describing cosmetics.[[428]](#cite_note-432) Faktorowicz is also credited with inventing modern [eyelash extensions](/wiki/Eyelash_extensions "Eyelash extensions").[[429]](#cite_note-433)[[430]](#cite_note-434) As of 2020, Poland possesses the sixth-largest cosmetic market in Europe. [Inglot Cosmetics](/wiki/Inglot_Cosmetics "Inglot Cosmetics") is the country's largest beauty products manufacturer,[[431]](#cite_note-435) and the retail store [Reserved](/wiki/Reserved "Reserved") is the country's most successful clothing store chain.[[432]](#cite_note-436)

Historically, fashion has been an important aspect of Poland's national consciousness or [cultural manifestation](/wiki/Culture_of_Poland "Culture of Poland"), beginning with the [poulaine](/wiki/Poulaine "Poulaine") pointed footwear in the 14th century, to developing an endemic style known as [Sarmatism](/wiki/Sarmatism "Sarmatism") at the turn of the 17th century.[[433]](#cite_note-Biedronska-Slota_2005-437) The national dress and etiquette of Poland reached the court at [Versailles](/wiki/Versailles "Versailles"), where French dresses inspired by Polish garments included *[robe à la polonaise](/wiki/Polonaise_(clothing) "Polonaise (clothing)")* and the [witzchoura](/wiki/Witzchoura "Witzchoura"). The scope of influence also entailed furniture; rococo [Polish beds](/wiki/Polish_bed "Polish bed") with [canopies](/wiki/Canopy_bed "Canopy bed") became fashionable in châteaus.[[434]](#cite_note-438) Sarmatism eventually faded in the wake of the 18th century.[[433]](#cite_note-Biedronska-Slota_2005-437)

### Cinema

Main article: [Cinema of Poland](/wiki/Cinema_of_Poland "Cinema of Poland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/Andrzej_Wajda_1974.jpg/250px-Andrzej_Wajda_1974.jpg)](/wiki/File:Andrzej_Wajda_1974.jpg)

[Andrzej Wajda](/wiki/Andrzej_Wajda "Andrzej Wajda") (1926–2016), renowned Polish film director

The [cinema of Poland](/wiki/Cinema_of_Poland "Cinema of Poland") traces its origins to 1894, when inventor [Kazimierz Prószyński](/wiki/Kazimierz_Pr%C3%B3szy%C5%84ski "Kazimierz Prószyński") patented the [Pleograph](/wiki/Pleograph "Pleograph") and subsequently the [Aeroscope](/wiki/Aeroscope "Aeroscope"), the first successful hand-held operated film camera.[[435]](#cite_note-Ford_2009-439)[[436]](#cite_note-Haltof_2015-440) In 1897, [Jan Szczepanik](/wiki/Jan_Szczepanik "Jan Szczepanik") constructed the [Telectroscope](/wiki/Telectroscope "Telectroscope"), a prototype of television transmitting images and sounds.[[435]](#cite_note-Ford_2009-439) They are both recognised as pioneers of [cinematography](/wiki/Cinematography "Cinematography").[[435]](#cite_note-Ford_2009-439) Poland has also produced influential directors, film producers and actors, many of whom were active in [Hollywood](/wiki/Hollywood,_Los_Angeles "Hollywood, Los Angeles"), chiefly [Roman Polański](/wiki/Roman_Pola%C5%84ski "Roman Polański"), [Andrzej Wajda](/wiki/Andrzej_Wajda "Andrzej Wajda"), [Pola Negri](/wiki/Pola_Negri "Pola Negri"), [Samuel Goldwyn](/wiki/Samuel_Goldwyn "Samuel Goldwyn"), the [Warner brothers](/wiki/Warner_Brothers "Warner Brothers"), [Max Fleischer](/wiki/Max_Fleischer "Max Fleischer"), [Agnieszka Holland](/wiki/Agnieszka_Holland "Agnieszka Holland"), [Krzysztof Zanussi](/wiki/Krzysztof_Zanussi "Krzysztof Zanussi") and [Krzysztof Kieślowski](/wiki/Krzysztof_Kie%C5%9Blowski "Krzysztof Kieślowski").[[437]](#cite_note-441)

The [themes](/wiki/Film_genre "Film genre") commonly explored in Polish cinema include [history](/wiki/Historical_film "Historical film"), [drama](/wiki/Drama_(film_and_television) "Drama (film and television)"), war, culture and black realism ([film noir](/wiki/Film_noir "Film noir")).[[435]](#cite_note-Ford_2009-439)[[436]](#cite_note-Haltof_2015-440) In the 21st-century, two Polish productions won the [Academy Awards](/wiki/Academy_Awards "Academy Awards") – *[The Pianist](/wiki/The_Pianist_(2002_film) "The Pianist (2002 film)")* (2002) by Roman Polański and *[Ida](/wiki/Ida_(film) "Ida (film)")* (2013) by [Paweł Pawlikowski](/wiki/Pawe%C5%82_Pawlikowski "Paweł Pawlikowski").[[436]](#cite_note-Haltof_2015-440) Polish cinematography also created many well-received comedies. The most known of them were made by [Stanisław Bareja](/wiki/Stanis%C5%82aw_Bareja "Stanisław Bareja") and [Juliusz Machulski](/wiki/Juliusz_Machulski "Juliusz Machulski").

### Media

Main article: [Mass media in Poland](/wiki/Mass_media_in_Poland "Mass media in Poland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/Siedziba_Telewizji_Polskiej_w_Warszawie_2017.jpg/250px-Siedziba_Telewizji_Polskiej_w_Warszawie_2017.jpg)](/wiki/File:Siedziba_Telewizji_Polskiej_w_Warszawie_2017.jpg)

Headquarters of the publicly funded national television network [TVP](/wiki/Telewizja_Polska "Telewizja Polska") in Warsaw

According to the [Eurobarometer Report](/wiki/Eurobarometer "Eurobarometer") (2015), 78 percent of Poles watch the [television](/wiki/Television_in_Poland "Television in Poland") daily.[[438]](#cite_note-Stepinska_2020-442) In 2020, 79 percent of the population read the news more than once a day, placing it second behind Sweden.[[439]](#cite_note-443) Poland has a number of major domestic media outlets, chiefly the [public broadcasting](/wiki/Public_broadcasting "Public broadcasting") corporation [TVP](/wiki/Telewizja_Polska "Telewizja Polska"), [free-to-air](/wiki/Free-to-air "Free-to-air") channels [TVN](/wiki/TVN_(Polish_TV_channel) "TVN (Polish TV channel)") and [Polsat](/wiki/Polsat "Polsat") as well as 24-hour news channels [TVP Info](/wiki/TVP_Info "TVP Info"), [TVN 24](/wiki/TVN_24 "TVN 24") and [Polsat News](/wiki/Polsat_News "Polsat News").[[440]](#cite_note-444) Public television extends its operations to genre-specific programmes such as [TVP Sport](/wiki/TVP_Sport "TVP Sport"), [TVP Historia](/wiki/TVP_Historia "TVP Historia"), [TVP Kultura](/wiki/TVP_Kultura "TVP Kultura"), [TVP Rozrywka](/wiki/TVP_Rozrywka "TVP Rozrywka"), TVP Seriale and [TVP Polonia](/wiki/TVP_Polonia "TVP Polonia"), the latter a state-run channel dedicated to the transmission of Polish-language telecasts for the [Polish diaspora](/wiki/Polish_diaspora "Polish diaspora"). In 2020, the most popular types of newspapers were [tabloids](/wiki/Tabloid_(newspaper_format) "Tabloid (newspaper format)") and socio-political news dailies.[[438]](#cite_note-Stepinska_2020-442)

Poland is a major European hub for video game developers and among the most successful companies are [CD Projekt](/wiki/CD_Projekt "CD Projekt"), [Techland](/wiki/Techland "Techland"), [The Farm 51](/wiki/The_Farm_51 "The Farm 51"), [CI Games](/wiki/CI_Games "CI Games") and [People Can Fly](/wiki/People_Can_Fly "People Can Fly").[[441]](#cite_note-Marszałkowski_2021-445) Some of the popular video games developed in Poland include *[The Witcher](/wiki/The_Witcher_(video_game_series) "The Witcher (video game series)")* trilogy and *[Cyberpunk 2077](/wiki/Cyberpunk_2077 "Cyberpunk 2077")*.[[441]](#cite_note-Marszałkowski_2021-445) The Polish city of [Katowice](/wiki/Katowice "Katowice") also hosts [Intel Extreme Masters](/wiki/Intel_Extreme_Masters "Intel Extreme Masters"), one of the biggest [esports](/wiki/Esports "Esports") events in the world.[[441]](#cite_note-Marszałkowski_2021-445)

### Sports

Main articles: [Sport in Poland](/wiki/Sport_in_Poland "Sport in Poland") and [Poland at the Olympics](/wiki/Poland_at_the_Olympics "Poland at the Olympics")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f8/National_Stadium_Warsaw_aerial_view_2.jpg/250px-National_Stadium_Warsaw_aerial_view_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:National_Stadium_Warsaw_aerial_view_2.jpg)

The [Kazimierz Górski National Stadium](/wiki/Kazimierz_G%C3%B3rski_National_Stadium "Kazimierz Górski National Stadium") in Warsaw, home of the [national football team](/wiki/Poland_national_football_team "Poland national football team")

[Motorcycle speedway](/wiki/Motorcycle_speedway "Motorcycle speedway"), volleyball and association football are among the country's most popular sports, with a rich history of international competitions.[[442]](#cite_note-FIFA_World_Cup-446)[[443]](#cite_note-FIFA_Statistics-447) [Track and field](/wiki/Track_and_field_athletics "Track and field athletics"), basketball, [handball](/wiki/Handball "Handball"), boxing, [MMA](/wiki/Mixed_martial_arts "Mixed martial arts"), [ski jumping](/wiki/Ski_jumping "Ski jumping"), [cross-country skiing](/wiki/Cross-country_skiing_(sport) "Cross-country skiing (sport)"), [ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey"), tennis, fencing, swimming, and [weightlifting](/wiki/Olympic_weightlifting "Olympic weightlifting") are other popular sports.
The golden era of [football in Poland](/wiki/Football_in_Poland "Football in Poland") occurred throughout the 1970s and went on until the early 1980s when the [Polish national football team](/wiki/Poland_national_football_team "Poland national football team") achieved their best results in any FIFA World Cup competitions finishing third place in [the 1974](/wiki/1974_FIFA_World_Cup "1974 FIFA World Cup") and [the 1982](/wiki/1982_FIFA_World_Cup "1982 FIFA World Cup") tournaments. The team won a gold medal [in football](/wiki/Football_at_the_Summer_Olympics "Football at the Summer Olympics") at the [1972 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1972_Summer_Olympics "1972 Summer Olympics") and two silver medals, [in 1976](/wiki/1976_Summer_Olympics "1976 Summer Olympics") and [in 1992](/wiki/1992_Summer_Olympics "1992 Summer Olympics"). In 2012, Poland co-hosted the [UEFA European Football Championship](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2012 "UEFA Euro 2012").[[444]](#cite_note-warsaw-life-448)

As of September 2024, the [Polish men's national volleyball team](/wiki/Poland_men%27s_national_volleyball_team "Poland men's national volleyball team") is ranked [as first](/wiki/FIVB_World_Rankings "FIVB World Rankings") in the world.[[445]](#cite_note-449) The team won a gold medal at the [1976 Summer Olympics](/wiki/Volleyball_at_the_1976_Summer_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_tournament "Volleyball at the 1976 Summer Olympics – Men's tournament") and the gold medal at the [FIVB World Championship](/wiki/FIVB_Volleyball_Men%27s_World_Championship "FIVB Volleyball Men's World Championship") [1974](/wiki/1974_FIVB_Volleyball_Men%27s_World_Championship "1974 FIVB Volleyball Men's World Championship"), [2014](/wiki/2014_FIVB_Volleyball_Men%27s_World_Championship "2014 FIVB Volleyball Men's World Championship") and [2018](/wiki/2018_FIVB_Volleyball_Men%27s_World_Championship "2018 FIVB Volleyball Men's World Championship").[[446]](#cite_note-450)[[447]](#cite_note-451)
[Mariusz Pudzianowski](/wiki/Mariusz_Pudzianowski "Mariusz Pudzianowski") is a highly successful strongman competitor and has won more [World's Strongest Man](/wiki/World%27s_Strongest_Man "World's Strongest Man") titles than any other competitor in the world, winning the event in 2008 for the fifth time.[[448]](#cite_note-452)

Poland has made a distinctive mark [in motorcycle speedway racing](/wiki/Speedway_in_Poland "Speedway in Poland"). The top [Ekstraliga](/wiki/Speedway_Ekstraliga "Speedway Ekstraliga") division has one of [the highest average attendances](/wiki/List_of_attendance_figures_at_domestic_professional_sports_leagues#Outdoor_sports "List of attendance figures at domestic professional sports leagues") for any sport in Poland. The [national speedway team of Poland](/wiki/Poland_national_speedway_team "Poland national speedway team") is one of the major teams in international speedway. Individually, Poland has three [Speedway Grand Prix](/wiki/Speedway_Grand_Prix "Speedway Grand Prix") World Champions, with the most successful being five-time World Champion [Bartosz Zmarzlik](/wiki/Bartosz_Zmarzlik "Bartosz Zmarzlik") who won back-to-back championships in 2019 and 2020 as well as 2022, 2023 and 2024. In 2021, Poland finished runners-up in the Speedway of Nations world championship final, held in [Manchester](/wiki/Manchester "Manchester"), England in 2021.[[449]](#cite_note-Speedway-453)

In the 21st century, the country has seen a growth of popularity of tennis and produced a number of successful tennis players including World No. 1 [Iga Świątek](/wiki/Iga_%C5%9Awi%C4%85tek "Iga Świątek"), winner of five [Grand Slam](/wiki/Grand_Slam_(tennis) "Grand Slam (tennis)") singles titles; former World No. 2 [Agnieszka Radwanska](/wiki/Agnieszka_Radwanska "Agnieszka Radwanska"), winner of 20 WTA career singles titles including [2015 WTA Finals](/wiki/2015_WTA_Finals_%E2%80%93_Singles "2015 WTA Finals – Singles"); Top 10 ATP player [Hubert Hurkacz](/wiki/Hubert_Hurkacz "Hubert Hurkacz"); former World No. 1 doubles player [Łukasz Kubot](/wiki/%C5%81ukasz_Kubot "Łukasz Kubot"), winner of two Grand Slam doubles titles and [Jan Zieliński](/wiki/Jan_Zieli%C5%84ski "Jan Zieliński"), winner of two Grand Slam mixed doubles titles. Poland also won the [2015 Hopman Cup](/wiki/2015_Hopman_Cup "2015 Hopman Cup") with Agnieszka Radwańska and [Jerzy Janowicz](/wiki/Jerzy_Janowicz "Jerzy Janowicz") representing the country.[[450]](#cite_note-454)[[451]](#cite_note-455)

Poles made significant achievements in mountaineering, in particular, in the [Himalayas](/wiki/Himalayas "Himalayas") and the winter ascending of the [eight-thousanders](/wiki/Eight-thousander "Eight-thousander") (e.g. [Jerzy Kukuczka](/wiki/Jerzy_Kukuczka "Jerzy Kukuczka"), [Krzysztof Wielicki](/wiki/Krzysztof_Wielicki "Krzysztof Wielicki"), [Wanda Rutkiewicz](/wiki/Wanda_Rutkiewicz "Wanda Rutkiewicz")). Polish mountains are one of the tourist attractions of the country. Hiking, climbing, skiing and mountain biking and attract numerous tourists every year from all over the world.[[292]](#cite_note-UNTWO_2008-296) Water sports are the most popular summer recreation activities, with ample locations for fishing, canoeing, kayaking, sailing and windsurfing especially in the northern regions of the country.[[452]](#cite_note-456)
